entry.title: SAP EHS Public Cloud: Extending Your Existing Landscape Side-by-Side - Guidebook (Part 4)
entry.content:
Integrating SAP EHS Public Cloud: Extending Your Existing Landscape Side-by-Side - Guidebook

Part 4: Integration of Functional Locations & Equipment from SAP S/4HANA On-Premise

Table of Contents:

  • 1. Why is this Integration required?
  • 2. Prerequisites
  • 3. Technical Integration
  • 3.1 SAP S/4HANA On-Premise Configuration
  • 3.2 SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Configuration
  • 3.2.1 Create Communication User
  • 3.2.2 Create Communication System
  • 3.2.3 Create Communication Arrangement
  • 3.3 SAP Integration Suite Configuration
  • 3.3.1Establishing the Event Queue for On-Premise Triggers
  • 3.3.2 Integration Credential Configuration
  • 3.3.3 Integration Flow (iFlow) Setup - Equipment
  • 3.3.4 Integration Flow (iFlow) Setup – Functional Locations
  • 3.4 Monitoring
  • 3.4.1 SAP S/4HANA On-Premise
  • 3.4.2 SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
  • 3.4.3 SAP Integration Suite
  • 4. Lessons Learned
  • 5. Engage with Us

Related Content:

Below is an overview of the planned blog series – each post focusing on a specific integration topic. Once published, the titles will be linked for direct access:

Integrating SAP EHS in the Public Cloud: Extending Your Existing Landscape Side-by-Side – Guidebook

Part 1: Introduction & OverviewLink
Part 2: Integration of Employees from SAP SuccessFactorsLink
Part 3: Integration of Business Partners & Material from SAP Master Data Governance (MDG)Link
Part 4: Integration of Functional Locations & Equipment from SAP S/4HANA On-Premise SystemLink
Part 5: Extensibility Scenarios & Further ImplementationLink
Part 6: Summary & OutlookLink

 

1. Why is this Integration required?

Integrating Functional Locations and Equipment master data from SAP S/4HANA On-Premise into SAP EHS Public Cloud is a foundational step for enabling core EHS processes such as Risk Management, Incident Management, and Emissions Management. Instead of maintaining asset-related data manually within SAP EHS Public Cloud, the system relies on validated Plant Maintenance (PM) master data coming from the customer’s existing ERP landscape. This ensures that all structures, machines, and technical objects involved in EHS processes remain consistent, reliable, and audit-proof across the enterprise.

Accurate and up-to-date Functional Location and Equipment data is essential because many EHS processes depend on a clear understanding of where work is performed and which technical objects is affected. This includes:

  • Identifying the exact machine or installation involved in an incident
  • Linking hazards to the correct technical asset in Risk Assessments
  • Performing control inspections and assigning follow-up actions
  • Ensuring asset-specific exposure, material usage, or emission characteristics are correctly evaluated
  • Centralizing reporting across operational sites based on real asset hierarchies

In other words: Without integrated Functional Location and Equipment data, SAP EHS Public Cloud cannot reliably assess risks, evaluate hazards, track incidents, or produce compliant operational reports.

Architectural Approach - Scope

The highlighted section of the architectural diagram illustrates the scope relevant to this blog post – focusing on the integration of Functional Locations and Equipment from SAP S/4HANA On-Premise into SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

Reference to User Scenario (B) – Risk Management

In part 1 “Introduction & Overview”, the User Scenario (B) – Risk Management at Mike Machineries (chapter 2.1.1) demonstrated why this integration is required:

Scenario Step

Why Plant Maintenance (PM) Integration Matters

A supervisor identifies a hazard in the chemical handling area

The hazard must be assigned to the correct Functional Location to ensure accurate traceability and reporting.

An Industrial Hygienist conducts a risk assessment

Equipment properties (e.g. ventilation units, storage cabinets, extraction systems) must be available from SAP PM to evaluate exposure and technical conditions.

Mitigation actions are defined

Some mitigation measures depend on technical object (e.g., upgrading ventilation, replacing equipment, updating signage).

A control inspection is performed

The inspection must reference the exact equipment or Functional Location where the mitigation is implemented.

Residual risk is recalculated

The correct asset hierarchy ensures that results are assigned to the right operational area or machine.

Results are shared with operations teams

Consistent Functional Location data ensures findings reach the correct plant, work center, or technical area.

Without integrated Functional Locations and Equipment, the system would not be able to:

  • Assign hazards or incidents to the correct asset
  • Trace risk assessments and inspections to the real technical environment
  • Evaluate machine-specific conditions and exposures
  • Monitor the effectiveness of mitigation actions at asset level
  • Ensure accurate reporting across operational areas and sites

Note: When implementing Risk Management as demonstrated in the user scenario, ensure that the relevant scope item 54A – Health & Safety Management (for Risk Management) is activated in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud via Central Business Configuration (CBC). This activation enables the Risk Assessment and Control Inspection processes in SAP EHS Public Cloud so that synchronized Functional Locations and Equipment master data can be used consistently.

While this integration is mandatory for Risk Management, it is equally relevant for other EHS use cases such as Incident Management or Emissions Management. In all these processes, consistent asset data provides the foundation for identifying affected areas, assessing technical conditions, and enabling accurate regulatory reporting.

 

2. Prerequisites

To successfully integrate Plant Maintenance data from SAP S/4HANA On-Premise into SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud via the SAP Integration Suite, the following prerequisites must be fulfilled:

Tenant Provisioning:  

  • SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud tenant 
  • SAP S/4HANA On-Premise tenant (with activated Plant Maintenance)
  • SAP Integration Suite tenant (Cloud Integration + Event Mesh)

Administrator Access: 

Ensure administrator users exist in all three systems: 

  • SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud: The administrator should have the template role SAP_BR_ADMINISTRATOR assigned to allow the creation of communication users, systems, and arrangements. 
  • SAP S/4HANA On-Premise: Users with access to the SPRO are required.
  • SAP Integration Suite: A User with admin rights for Event Mesh and iFlow deployment is required.

Scope Item Activation:  

Following scope items need to be activated in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud:

  • 4HH – Reactive Maintenance (mandatory for the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud communication scenario regarding the inbound services for Equipment and Functional Locations).
  • 54A – Health & Safety Management (required for Risk Management scenario).

Activated OData Services in the SAP S/4HANA On-Premise System:

  • API_EQUIPMENT and API_FUNCTIONALLOCATION active in SAP Gateway (check via /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE).

Event Mesh Instance:

  • Provisioned by SAP BTP with service key available (for AMQP connection).

These prerequisites ensure that the integration of Plant Maintenance – specially changes made in SAP S/4HANA On-Premise system – operates smoothly and reliably, thereby supporting EHS processes such as Risk Management.

 

3. Technical Integration

This section provides an overview of the technical integration required to replicate Plant Maintenance master data from SAP S/4HANA On-Premise to SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud for EHS processes.

The integration scenario builds on the activation of the scope item 4HH – Reactive Maintenance, which delivers the necessary inbound services for Equipment and Functional Locations in the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. These objects form the foundation for EHS capabilities such as Risk Management, Incident Management, or Emissions Management.

The process shown in this integration scenario is event-driven. This means, whenever changes occur to Plant Maintenance master data, such as Equipment or Functional Location in the On-Premise system, these updates are automatically replicated to the Public Cloud. This approach keeps both systems aligned without manual steps or scheduled batch jobs, ensuring that asset information is always current.

Keeping master data synchronized between both systems is critical for effective Risk Management because risk assessments and mitigation strategies depend on accurate details about assets and their locations.

By ensuring that updates flow seamlessly between systems, organizations can make informed decisions and maintain compliance across their EHS processes.

Key Configuration Areas: 

  • SAP S/4HANA On-Premise Configuration 
  • SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Configuration 
  • SAP Integration Suite Configuration
  • Monitoring

3.1 SAP S/4HANA On-Premise Configuration

As this integration scenario for Functional Locations and Equipment is event driven, the creation of channels was required in the SAP S/4HANA On-Premise system to publish business events to the SAP Integration Suite via Event Mesh.

In this context the channels define the protocol (AMQP), destination (Event Mesh service key), and topic space.

Outbound bindings specify which event topics (e.g., sap/s4/beh/equipment/v1/Equipment/*) are sent. This ensures that changes in Functional Locations or Equipment trigger the corresponding iFlows in the SAP Integration Suite.

Channels can be created by navigating to SPRO > ABAP Platform > Enterprise Event Enablement > Administration > Chanel Connection Settings > Manage Channels and Parameters.

Therefore, we created the channel IS_INTERNAL_EVENTMESH which uses the AMQP Protocol.

Event Channels in SAP S/4HANA On-Premise

After creating the channel, we added two outbound bindings to define which event topic should be published.

  • sap/s4/beh/equipment/v1/Equipment/*
  • sap/s4/beh/functionallocation/v1/FunctionalLocation/*

Added Outbound Bindings to created Event Channel

This configuration ensures that business events for Functional Locations and Equipment are reliably published from the on-premise system to SAP Integration Suite via Event Mesh, enabling event-driven iFlows without the need for polling.

3.2 SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Configuration

In addition to setting up SAP S/4HANA On-Premise, the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud must also be configured to receive employee master data via the SAP Integration Suite. 

For this purpose, a Communication Arrangement is required to enable secure data exchange between the systems. In this scenario, the predefined Communication Arrangement SAP_COM_0395 – Asset Management Master Data Integration is used.

By activating and configuring this Communication Arrangement enables the necessary inbound API services, allowing the SAP Integration Suite to push employee data into SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. 

To create the Communication Arrangement, we need to create a Communication User as well as a Communication System that will be assigned to the related Communication Arrangement.

3.2.1 Create Communication User

In this integration scenario, an Inbound Communication User was created in the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud (e.g., user OPN_IN). This user was used for Basic Authentication in the Communication Arrangement, and its credentials were securely stored in the Security Material of the SAP Integration Suite to enable a safe connection between the systems. 

Select Maintain Communication Users

To create the Inbound Communication User in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, log in to the Fiori Launchpad as an administrator and open the app “Maintain Communication Users”.  

Select "New"

If no suitable user exists, select “New” to create one.

Created Communication User - General Data

The username and password for Basic Authentication can be freely defined. 

Related Communication System and Arrangements

Once created, the system automatically structures the user view, showing assignments to Communication Systems and Arrangements (added in later steps). 

3.2.2 Create Communication System

The Communication System is a necessary component for the Communication Arrangement. If you have already set up a Communication System for the S/4HANA On-Premise system, you can use it for the Communication Arrangement. Please note that the previously created Communication User must be assigned to this system for the following steps. Furthermore, no additional system-level settings (regarding outbound communication) are necessary, as the communication scenario SAP_COM_0395 only uses inbound services.

If no related user exists, you can create a new one as follows: Navigate to the S/4HANA Public Cloud app “Communication Systems”.

Select Communication Systems

Select “New”.

Select "New"

Enter a System ID and System Name referring to the SAP S/4HANA On-Premise tenant and proceed with the set up. 

Creating new Communication System

Select Inbound Only as the communication scenario SAP_COM_0395 only uses Inbound Communication.

Select Inbound Only

Assign the previously created Communication User.

Assign Communication User

3.2.3 Create Communication Arrangement

The final step in the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud configuration is to bind the previously created components  (Communication User and Communication System) within the Communication Arrangement (SAP_COM_0395 – Asset Management Master Data Integration). 

Once configured, SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud is ready to receive replicated Plant Maintenance master data (e. g. Functional Locations or Equipment) from the SAP S/4HANA On-Premise system through the SAP Integration Suite. The Communication Arrangement provides the inbound service URLs, which must be stored in the corresponding Integration Flow (iFlow) of the SAP Integration Suite to establish end-to-end communication. 

To create the arrangement, log in to the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Fiori Launchpad as an administrator and open the “Communication Arrangements” app. 

Select Communication Arrangements

Then click on “New” to create a new Communication Arrangement. 

Select "New"

Select Communication Scenario SAP_COM_0395

Choose SAP_COM_0395 as the Communication Scenario.

Choose Communication Scenario SAP_COM_0395

After the creation of the Communication Arrangement the before created Communication System will be attached. Note that the corresponding Communication User will be determined automatically.

Communication System and User

The Inbound Service of the Communication Arrangement provides us then the Service URL, that will be used as the receiver endpoint in the iFlow configuration of the SAP Integration Suite.  

Required Inbound Services

3.3 SAP Integration Suite Configuration

In this integration scenario we used the SAP Integration Suite as the middleware layer to enable secure and reliable data exchange between the SAP S/4HANA On-Premise system and SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. Therefore, the SAP Integration Suite is responsible for retrieving Plant Maintenance master data (such as Functional Locations and Equipment) from the On-Premise system and delivering it to the Public Cloud using event-driven integration flows.

Since there is no pre-delivered integration package available for this scenario, we created two custom iFlows to handle the replication of Functional Location and Equipment data. Both iFlows follow a similar structure in terms of processing logic; however, they differ in the endpoints used for communication with SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and the On-Premise system respectively.

Custom Integration Package for this Scenario

3.3.1 Establishing the Event Queue for On-Premise Triggers

The first step in this implementation is to create an Event Queue in SAP Event Mesh using the Message Client. This queue serves as the trigger point for event-driven integration. When a change occurs in the SAP S/4HANA On-Premise system (such as an update to Functional Locations or Equipment), an event is published to the queue.

The iFlow will be later subscribed to the Event Queue and use the event as a trigger to start processing. Once triggered, the iFlow calls the related API in the On-Premise system to retrieve the full master data for the affected object. This design ensures that updates are replicated to the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud in near real-time, keeping both systems synchronized.

Note: The SAP Event Mesh – Message client is typically provisioned by the BASIS administrator. If you have no existing Message Client, please refer to your BASIS team.

Once the message client was set up, navigate to it inside the SAP Integration Suite > Configure > Event Mesh.

Navigate to Message Client in SAP Integration Suite

Since our EHS scenario relates to Functional Locations and Equipment, we have created two separate event queues here. Therefore, select Create.

Select "Create"

Enter a name for the new Event Queue(e. g. opn.equipment). You can keep the remaining fields as default.

Queue Settings for opn.equipment

Then navigate to Subscriptions and select Create.

Select "Create"

Two Topic Subscriptions were required within the Event Queue Details for the Equipment:

  • <message-client-prefix>/ce/sap/s4/beh/equipment/v1/Equipment/Created/v1
  • <message-client-prefix>/de/ce/sap/s4/beh/equipment/v1/Equipment/Changed/v1

Note: Regarding the Subscription, the message-client-prefix will be typically provisioned by your BASIS team. The remaining part of the respective topic names of the Equipment event is SAP standard and can be viewed in the SAP Business Accelerator Hub in the tab Event References Event References | Equipment Events | SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

Repeat this step also for the Functional Location. Therefore again, enter a name for the new event queue (e. g. opn.FuncLoc). The following subscriptions are needed for this event queue:

  • <message-client-prefix>/ce/sap/s4/beh/functionallocation/v1/FunctionalLocation/Changed/v1
  • <message-client-prefix>/ce/sap/s4/beh/functionallocation/v1/FunctionalLocation/Created/v1

The Functional Location events for the Functional Locations can be found in the SAP Business Accelerator Hub in the tab Event References Event References | Functional Location Events | SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

3.3.2 Integration Credential Configuration

Within the SAP Integration Suite, the created integration flows (iFlows) must authenticate against three endpoints:

  • SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud – to push the synchronized data to the cloud system.
  • SAP S/4HANA On-Premise – to retrieve Functional Location and Equipment data
  • SAP Event Mesh – to consume events via AMQP protocol and trigger the integration process.

Note: To ensure secure authentication for each endpoint used by the iFlow, a dedicated Security Material object was created for every connection. In our demo environment, Basic Authentication was sufficient; however, for productive landscapes, certificate-based authentication is required to meet security standards and comply with enterprise policies.

For communication with SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, Basic Authentication was used in this scenario. The credentials of the Inbound Communication User from SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud must therefore be maintained as Security Material within the SAP Integration Suite. This configuration ensures that the integration flow can authenticate securely when transmitting data to the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Security Material

The Security Material that was used for communication with the SAP S/4HANA On-Premise system, was created in a similar way using Basic Authentication, based on the credentials of the On-Premise user. Note that the On-Premise user will be provided by your SAP BASIS team.

SAP S/4HANA On-Premise Security Material

For the Event Mesh Security Material that enables communication with SAP Event Mesh, OAuth 2.0 was used for authentication. Therefore, enter the Token Service URL, Client ID and Client Secret, which are provided by the service key of your SAP Event Mesh Instance in SAP BTP Cockpit.

SAP Integration Suite - Event Mesh Security Material

3.3.3 Integration Flow (iFlow) Setup - Equipment

Regarding the iFlows we will setup the Equipment Integration iFlow first. Therefore, create the iFlow with a corresponding name, set SAP S/4HANA as Sender and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition as Receiver.

Sender and Receiver Setup for Equipment iFlow

The integration flow starts with an AMQP connection to SAP Event Mesh. This connection allows the iFlow to subscribe to event queues and consume messages whenever Functional Location or Equipment changes occur in the SAP S/4HANA On-Premise system. The AMQP protocol ensures reliable, event-driven communication between the systems and acts as the trigger for the subsequent OData calls.

Therefore, connect the Receiver with the Start Message using the Adapter Type AMQP with Websocket as Transport Protocol.

First iFlow Step - AMQP Adapter Setup

The General tab inside the Adapter can stay as default. In the Connection tab you need to add a Host, Port and Path of the Event Mesh Service Key. You will find this Service Key in the Event Mesh Instance of your SAP BTP Cockpit. For Authentication use OAuth 2.0 and add the associated Security Material for Event Mesh (created in the previous step) as Credential Name.

AMQP Adapter Connection Setup

AMQP Adapter Connection Setup

In the Processing tab, add the event queue name for the equipment.

Assign Event Queue opn.equipment to AMQP Adapter

The incoming event message from SAP Event Mesh is delivered in JSON format. Since subsequent processing steps and OData calls require XML, the JSON to XML Converter transforms the payload accordingly. In this scenario, namespace mapping is disabled because the JSON structure does not include namespaces that need to be preserved in the XML output. The remaining parts of the Converter can stay as default.

JSON to XML Converter

After the conversion to XML, we added a Content Modifier. This Content Modifier is used to extract specific values from the XML structure and store them as Message Header and Exchange Properties.

Content Modifier Setup - General

In the Message Header tab, the Content Modifier reads values from the XML payload using XPath and sets them as message headers. In our example, we set the SAP_ApplicationID header with the Functional Location value found at /root/data/Equipment in the XML.

Content Modifier Setup - Message Header

In the Exchange Property tab, the Content Modifier creates two properties by extracting values from the XML payload using XPath: p_eventType from /root/type and p_equipmentno from /root/data/Equipment. These properties are later used to dynamically build the OData request URL for the GET call to the on-premise system.

Content Modifier Setup - Exchange Property

Next, we set a Request Reply with the name Get Equipment Data. This enables us to get the data via OData Adapter from the SAP S/4HANA On-Premise system.

Request Reply with OData Adapter to SAP S/4HANA On-Premise System

In the Connection tab of the OData adapter, we define how the integration flow connects to the SAP S/4HANA On-Premise system. In our example, the address consists of the virtual host and port provided by the SAP Cloud Connector, followed by the OData service path /sap/opu/odata/sap/API_EQUIPMENT/, which is the standard endpoint for the Equipment API in SAP Gateway. The proxy type is set to “On-Premise”, and the Location ID refers to the configured Cloud Connector destination (can be found in the Master Instance for your Cloud Connector in the SAP BTP Cockpit > Logged in into your Subaccount > Connectivity > Cloud Connectors). Regarding the authentication, was used with the on-premise related Security Material.

Note: We leveraged an existing SAP Cloud Connector configuration to securely connect the SAP Integration Suite to the on-premise system without exposing the real backend host.

SAP S/4HANA On-Premise OData Adapter Setup - Connection

Note that the API_EQUIPMENT is activated in your on-premise system. You can check this via the transaction /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE and searching for the API_EQUIPMENT OData Service.  

In the Processing tab, we configured a GET operation to the Resource Path Equipment. Therefore, click Select.

SAP S/4HANA On-Premise OData Adapter Setup - Processing

Choose Connection Source Remote. The fields will be automatically filled with the provided data in the Connection tab. Then click on Step 2. If the connection is configured correctly, we will configure the operation and entities here.

SAP S/4HANA On-Premise OData Adapter Setup - Processing - Resource Path Configuration 1

Then select the operation “GET”, the Select Entity “Equipment”, the Sub Levels 1 and choose “Select All Fields”.

SAP S/4HANA On-Premise OData Adapter Setup - Processing - Resource Path Configuration 2

To retrieve only the changes for the specific Equipment Number, apply a filter on the property ${property.p_equipmentno} that was set in the Content Modifier earlier. Filter for the ValidityEndDate as shown below.

SAP S/4HANA On-Premise OData Adapter Setup - Processing - Resource Path Configuration 3

SAP S/4HANA On-Premise OData Adapter Setup - Processing - Resource Path Configuration 4

After retrieving the equipment data from the SAP S/4HANA On-Premise system, we ensured with a message mapping step that the structure and fields of the incoming data match with the expected values for the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. For our related message mapping we used a predefined mapping resource (/MM.mmap) created in the iFlow to transform the received data to the expected structure.

Message Mapping 1

Message Mapping 2

After completing the mapping as required for the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Equipment Inbound Service, we set a new Request Reply with an OData V2 adapter to the receiver endpoint of the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

Request Reply with OData Adapter to SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud System

In the Connection tab add the inbound service URL for the Equipment provided by the Communication Arrangement SAP_COM_0395 in the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

Inbound Service URL for Equipment in Communication Arrangement SAP_COM_0395

Choose Proxy Type Internet and add your Security Material that was created for communication with the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud via Basic Authentication.

As we want to send the structure which the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud system expects, we need to create new processing details for this OData Adapter. Therefore, choose the Processing tab and navigate to Select.

SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud OData Adapter Setup - Processing

Choose again the Connection Source Remote. The required details to connect with the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud system will be shown here again automatically after the previous details in the Connection tab have been configured. Then navigate to Step 2.

SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud OData Adapter Setup - Processing - Resource Path Configuration 1

Now choose the Operation Post, Entity Equipment, Sub Levels 1 and Select All Fields. For the Post operation no filters are required.

SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud OData Adapter Setup - Processing - Resource Path Configuration 2

Note: The iFlow also shows an Exception Subprocess. You can perform this after the implementation of the iFlow is finished. In our scenario we let the Exception Subprocess empty.

Exception Subprocess

3.3.4 Integration Flow (iFlow) Setup – Functional Locations

The iFlow for the Functional Locations was similar in architecture, with small changes.

Created iFlow for Functional Location

For the AMQP Adapter at beginning of the iFlow, the event queue for Functional Location was used.

Used Event Queue in iFlow Funcional Location

According to Content Modifier > Message Header, the used Source Value was /root/data/FunctionalLocation.

Content Modifier in iFlow Functional Location - Message Header

In the Exchange Property tab of the Content Modifier, we changed the second line entry to the Name p_functionallocation and its Source Value to /root/data/FunctionalLocation.

Content Modifier in iFlow Functional Location - Exchange Property

According to the Get Functional Location Data that will perform a GET operation to the SAP S/4HANA On-Premise system, we used the OData Service for “API_FUNCTIONALLOCATION” with the root address /sap/opu/odata/sap/API_FUNCTIONALLOCATION/. Note again that the API_FUNCTIONALLOCATION is activated in your on-premise system. You can check this via the transaction /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE and searching for the API_FUNCTIONALLOCATION OData Service. 

Request Reply with OData Adapter to SAP S/4HANA On-Premise System

In the Processing tab navigate to Select and go to Step 2 (As the fields required for the system connection will be filled automatically).

Choose the Operation and Select Entity as shown below with Sub Level 1 and Select All Fields.

SAP S/4HANA On-Premise OData Adapter Setup - Processing - Resource Path Configuration

Regarding the query, we were only filtering for FunctionalLocationLabelName equals ${property.p_functionallocation}.

The Message Mapping now needs to be performed for the Functional Location. Therefore, we used again the Resource /MM.mmap but now with a new mapping.

Message Mapping 1

Message Mapping 2

For the last OData Adapter sending the transformed Functional Location data from the SAP Integration Suite to the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, the Inbound Service URL for the Functional Location was used in the Address Field of the Connection Details.

Request Reply with OData Adapter to SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud System - Connection

Inbound Service URL for Equipment in Communication Arrangement SAP_COM_0395

According to the Processing details, the connection to the system will be again filled automatically. Then Navigate to Step 2 and choose the Select Entity as well as the Operation as shown below. Choose again Select All Fields.

SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud OData Adapter Setup - Processing - Resource Path Configuration

3.4 Monitoring

Monitoring is a critical part of ensuring that event-driven integration for EHS Risk Management runs reliably across SAP S/4HANA (Public Cloud & On-Premise) and the SAP Integration Suite. Accurate and timely event processing is essential because many risk-related processes depend on these events. Missing or delayed events can lead to incomplete risk assessments, gaps in safety measures, and potential compliance violations.

The Monitoring can be performed at all three involved systems.

3.4.1 SAP S/4HANA On-Premise

Regarding the Outbound Events that are sent from the on-premise system to the SAP Integration Suite, you can navigate to SPRO > ABAP Platform > Enterprise Event Enablement > Monitoring > Monitor Event Queues.

If any failures for the Outbound Events occur, they can be analyzed here.

Event Monitor in SAP S/4HANA On-Premise System

3.4.2 SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, monitoring focuses on API communication rather than event queues. The primary tool that can be used here is the “OData Error Log” app, which provides detailed error messages for failed OData requests. This is essential for troubleshooting issues when SAP Integration Suite sends data to the Public Cloud system.

OData Error Log in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud System

3.4.3 SAP Integration Suite

SAP Integration Suite provides several tools to track and manage integrations flows. As already described already in the part 2 you can therefore navigate within the SAP Integration Suite to Monitor > Overview > Manage Integration Content. Here you can select your respective iFlow (e.g. “Functional Location Integration in between SAP S4HANA and SAP S4HANA Cloud”) and select Monitor Message Processing.

Manage Integration Content for Functional Location iFlow

This view provides a clear overview of the processing status for each message, indicating whether it was completed successfully or if any errors occurred during execution.

Monitor Message Processing

By selecting Message Status Overview on the upper right corner,  statistics will be shown across all processed messages, including completed, failed and retried executions. This provides a consolidated view of the integration scenario over a selected timeframe and supports quick identification of patterns or recurring issues. 

Message Status Overview

 

4. Lessons Learned

Implementing this integration scenario via the scope item 4HH for Plant Maintenance data (such as Functional Locations or Equipment) from the SAP S/4HANA On-Premise system to SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud provided several valuable insights that are highly relevant for similar integration projects.

1. Event-Driven Integration Ensures Real-Time Accuracy: Using SAP Event Mesh with AMQP is critical for keeping Functional Location and Equipment data synchronized. Batch jobs cannot guarantee timely updates for EHS processes like Risk Management.

2. Validate OData Services Early: Missing activation of API_EQUIPMENT or API_FUNCTIONALLOCATION in the on-premise system is a common root cause for iFlow failures. Always check via /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE before starting.

3. Topic Naming in Event Mesh Requires Precision: Incorrect prefixes or typos in topic subscriptions will block event processing entirely. Always cross-check against SAP Business Accelerator Hub references.

4. Message Mapping Is Compliance-Critical: Ensure that Equipment and Functional Location data is transformed correctly for SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. Missing mandatory fields can lead to incomplete risk assessments and reporting gaps.

5. Monitoring Is Essential for Operational Reliability: Event-driven integration depends on timely message processing. Use monitoring tools in all three systems (SAP S/4HANA On-Premise, SAP Integration Suite, and SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud) to detect and resolve issues early.

6. Plan Authentication Strategy Upfront: Basic Authentication works for demos, but production environments require certificate-based authentication. Delaying this switch can cause project delays and security risks.

 

5. Engage with Us

To follow the series, make sure to subscribe to the SAP EHS Public Cloud blog tag.

We welcome your thoughts and feedback in the comments!

Acknowledgements

This blog post series is the result of close collaboration within our EHS implementation team. I would like to express my sincere appreciation to my colleagues, whose expertise, insights, and project experience significantly contributed to the depth and quality of this guide.

A special thank you goes to:

  • @Westphal 
  • @qkroiss 
  • @asutoshmaharana2326 
  • @kanwal_singh
  • @Suraj_Ashray1 

Their contributions were instrumental in shaping the content of this blog post. I am truly grateful for their collaborations and support.

entry.title: What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2511
entry.content:
This November release is a bumper edition and the most significant of the year for SAP Asset Performance Management. It brings Embedded IoT out of early adopter access into general availability, introduces new applications, built using SAP's Cloud Application Programming (CAP) model, opening exciting extensibility possibilities for asset management. And there are great enhancements for rules, industrial systems and recommendations. Here's an overview:

  • Embedded IoT is now available to all customers and partner
  • Managing Industrial Systems and Industrial System Templates Just Got Easier
  • New CAP Applications for Risk & Criticality Applications
  • Assign monitoring rules directly to proactive recommendations with the Condition subtype.
  • Copy standalone recommendations from both the object and list pages.
  • Navigate directly to maintenance plans or task lists in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition.
  • Improvements to Rule Processing
    • Cleaner Data: Automatic Exclusion of Obsolete Technical Objects
    • Automatic Rule Triggering for New Measurement Data
  • Extensibility and Performance Improvements
  • Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition

Embedded IoT is now available to all customers and partners!

The centerpiece of this release is the general availability of Embedded IoT. Previously available only to early adopters, Embedded IoT now allows organizations to bring IoT device management and condition monitoring fully into SAP Asset Performance Management.

The launchpad of SAP Asset Performance Management now contains five new application tiles that are accessible to end-users depending on their assigned business roles. These tiles navigate directly to the respective applications in the embedded IoT platform:

You can:

  • Manage Devices – Onboard, configure, and control IoT devices across their lifecycle.
  • Map Devices to Technical Objects – Bring together operational technologies and IT technologies by linking devices and data points to technical objects and indicators for seamless integration.
  • Manage Streaming Rules – Process IoT signals and events in near real-time.
  • Manage IoT Dashboards – Central workspace for monitoring devices and visualizing insights.
  • IoT Platform Administration – Configure the embedded IoT platform, manage users, and control system-wide settings.

This brings true end-to-end asset monitoring into the hands of asset managers, with near real-time insights, automated rule triggering, and integrated dashboards for decision-making. For more information and videos, see the dedicated blog by Florian Weigold Embedded IoT is here: 5 apps that accelerate sensor to insight in SAP Asset Performance Management.

Managing Industrial Systems and Industrial System Templates Just Got Easier

See how the new 2511 features make a reliability engineer’s day smoother and more efficient.

Template Work: Reviewing and Updating

Your first job is to review an Industrial System Template with hundreds of elements. You open the Structure tab expecting the usual lag... but now, the Structure tab opens instantly in a new default table view for hierarchies with over three hundred elements providing a much better performance. A good start to the day! For more information, see Components of Industrial System Template Structure.

Soon after, a colleague asks who changed part of the template last week. With the new Change Logs , you find and export the full history in seconds. For more information, see View and Export Change Logs. Later, you want to make an update to the template, which is already released - something that wasn't possible before, now  - and even push the changes to all connected systems. 
A quick glance at the newly introduced Reference Designator Library Version in the table view confirms everything is aligned. 

By the end of the template review, you’ve worked faster, avoided workarounds, and ensured all templates are accurate and up to date.

Industrial System Work: Monitoring and Troubleshooting

After lunch, you move on to Industrial Systems. You create a new industrial system from a template: the structure stays fixed, while product aspects, descriptions, and organisational attributes remain editable. The new Synchronization Status instantly shows it’s up to date with the template.

Shortly after a technician calls: they need to take a pump sub-assembly offline for a quick check.
Before, you had to improvise workarounds. Now, you just deactivate the system element with one click. Once the check is complete, you reactivate it just as easily. For more information, see Activate or Deactivate a System Element.. 

Before you leave for the day you check the change logs for yesterday’s update — the pressure sensor mappings on Compressor 3 were adjusted in the control system during night shift, and you can see exactly who changed what and when.

Two minutes later, a colleague pings you “Can you check Compressor 3? The output readings don’t seem right.” You open the system’s structure — enormous with over three hundred interconnected elements that used to freeze your screen — but today, just like the template, it loads instantly in the optimized table view, so you can spot the mismatch immediately.

While you’re there, you quickly verify the reference designator library version to ensure the system is aligned with the latest engineering release. Everything matches.  You also check the Organizational Attributes in the system’s Excel export to confirm that ownership and responsibilities are up to date. For more information see Creating an Industrial System from Excel and Export or Import Sytem Structure

Thanks to the recent API migration from the Asset Health package to the Manage Asset Data package, your scripts and integrations run smoothly without any hiccups. For more information, refer to Manage Asset Data API

By the end of the day, you've worked faster, with more clarity and fewer workarounds — thanks to thoughtful enhancements across both templates and systems.

In a nutshell:

  • Industrial systems and templates can now display reference designator library versions, track change logs, and synchronize with templates for better alignment.
  • Large system hierarchies default to table view for faster navigation, improving usability in complex environments.
  • Released templates can be edited and changes pushed to associated systems, giving teams more flexibility to evolve structures without losing control.
  • System elements can now be activated or deactivated, and limited edits to systems created from templates are supported.
  • Together, these improvements make the platform more responsive, adaptable, and ready for advanced use cases.

We will be releasing a dedicated blog on industrial systems and template next week. Watch this space.

New CAP Applications for Risk & Criticality Applications

With the 2511 release, we’re introducing two new CAP-based applications: Manage Templates for Risk & Criticality Assessments and Manage Risk & Criticality Assessments. These CAP apps bring a more modern, scalable, and extensible architecture, delivering smoother performance and a more consistent user experience. 

You can enable the new CAP-based apps by adding the new authorization scope (RiskCriticality_CAP) to the users. Watch this space for a dedicated blog in the coming weeks.

For more information on release timelines, implementation, support, and known limitations, refer to SAP Note 3603407

Entry Point to New CAP-Based Applications for Risk and Criticality on SAP Fiori Launchpad

 Asset Reliability Engineering

Recommendations have become even more actionable:

Assign monitoring rules directly to proactive recommendations with the Condition subtype.

With the 2511 release, APM restores an important capability: assigning IoT monitoring rules to proactive condition-based recommendations. This was available in the earlier SAP IoT solution, but the new embedded IoT version delivers it on a stronger and more modern foundation - a more powerful rules application and a new, independent dataset application.

This evolved architecture makes the connection between IoT rules and APM recommendations cleaner, more flexible, and easier to extend than it was in sIoT. Instead of simply copying the old behaviour, APM now integrates with IoT rules in a way that matches the newer, more capable platform.

What problem are we solving?

Before the ability to assign eIoT monitoring rules, proactive condition-based recommendations were largely static. Maintenance teams could see a recommendation, but there was no automated way to know when it actually needed attention. Decisions about timing relied on manual monitoring, scheduled inspections, or guesswork, which could lead to reactive maintenance, unnecessary work, or missed opportunities to prevent failures. Recommendations lacked the proper context from real-time sensor data, making it difficult to prioritize actions effectively or ensure maintenance was truly condition-driven.

The new CAP recommendation app now includes a dedicated section for assigning embedded IoT monitoring rules. Once a rule is linked, APM can automatically react when that rule detects an unusual pattern in the sensor data.

Rule creation will follow in a future release. Rules - and their associated datasets — must be created separately in their own applications. 

For more information, refer to Implementing a Proactive Type Condition Recommendation.

Watch this quick demo:

Video demo showing how to assign monitoring rules to proactive condition-based recommendations in the new CAP-based Implement Recommendations app

UI improvements

Information for rules is now in the recommendation column of the Implement Recommendations list page. You can click on the rule(s) associated with the recommendation and see a popup, from where you can navigate directly to the Manage Rules and Manage Datasets apps.

UI enhancements for rules in the Implement Recommendations app

Copy standalone recommendations from both the object and list pages.

You can now copy a standalone recommendation with and without coding mask from both the object page and the list page in the Manage Recommendations app.

In many reliability and maintenance processes, teams often create multiple recommendations that are nearly identical, especially when working across similar assets, recurring failure modes, or repeated strategy decisions. Until now, every recommendation had to be created manually from scratch, even when most of the content already existed elsewhere.

The new Copy Recommendation function solves this problem. You take an existing standalone recommendation and create a new one with the same core information already prefilled. This includes the recommendation details and notes, but intentionally not the comments—because comments usually reflect a specific discussion on the original recommendation and shouldn’t be carried forward.

The copied recommendation is treated as a new object with no linkage or lineage to the source—just a convenient head start.

Business Value

  • Speed: Reduces effort when creating multiple similar recommendations.

  • Consistency: Ensures repeated recommendations follow the same structure and information.

  • Quality: Minimises manual entry errors by reusing accurate, validated data.

  • Scalability: Particularly valuable during large strategy projects, where dozens or hundreds of recommendations need to be generated efficiently.

For more information, refer to Copying a Recommendation.

Example: Copy recommendation with coding mask in Manage Recommendations app

Navigate directly to maintenance plans or task lists in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition.

To make maintenance workflows more efficient, we added direct navigation from recommendations to the related Task List or Maintenance Plan. Customers can configure the Launchpad URL for their environment, and the system generates links that open the correct object in the corresponding application.

This eliminates the need to search manually, saving time and reducing errors. Teams can move seamlessly from identifying an issue to implementing the required actions, improving efficiency and traceability across large maintenance projects.

To navigate to SAP S/4HANA system to view maintenance list details, you have configured the destination information in the BTP cockpit. For more information, see Creating a Maintenance Plan for Recommendation and Creating a Task List for Recommendation.

 

Improvements to Rule Processing

We have introduced two improvements to enhance the accuracy and automation of rule-based monitoring across SAP Asset Performance Management.

What Problem Are We Solving?

Until now, aggregation rules could be affected by irrelevant technical objects (for example, objects already inactive or marked for deletion) and sometimes required manual intervention to trigger rule execution when new measurement data arrived. This led to inconsistent results, unnecessary noise in aggregated values, and additional effort for reliability engineers.

What’s New

Cleaner Data: Automatic Exclusion of Obsolete Technical Objects

When creating new aggregation rules, child technical objects with the statuses  Inactive, Marked for Deletion, or Deleted are now automatically excluded.  This ensures that only valid and active parts of your asset structure contribute to aggregated calculations, resulting in more accurate monitoring and fewer irrelevant results. For more information, see Creating an Aggregation Rule.

Automatic Rule Triggering for New Measurement Data

Rules using the Measurement Document Ingestion trigger now run automatically when:

  • A measurement document is replicated from SAP S/4HANA
  • A new indicator value is created manually

This eliminates manual steps, ensures rules react immediately to fresh data, and helps reliability engineers maintain up-to-date insights without intervention. For more information, see Triggering a Rule.

 

Extensibility and Performance Improvements

With the new CAP applications, this also opens the door to greater extensibility, allowing organizations to adapt applications and processes to their unique needs. Watch this space for a dedicated blog on how to configure the CAP apps to enable extensibility. Coming soon!

Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition

The 2025 release of SAP S/4HANA Cloud, private edition brings deeper integration with SAP Asset Performance Management (APM). APM recommendations, indicators, and assessments are now visible directly in the Find Technical Objects app, together with risk and criticality information. In addition, APM failure data relationships can now be used to select Failure Modes and Failure Effects during maintenance processes, ensuring the right task lists are assigned and actions are aligned with historical recommendations.

For full details, read Fernanda Rodrigues' blog Asset Management in SAP Cloud ERP Private 2025 Release and explore Fernanda's other blogs too.

Stay Informed and Get Involved

  • Find out what's up and coming in the SAP Road Map Explorer.
  • For an exhaustive list of all new features, visit the SAP Help Portal - What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management.. 
  • Send us your improvement requests. Submit or vote for the improvement you would like to see in the SAP Continuous Influence session.
  • Check the APM link collection. 

Let us know what you think about these new features - leave us a comment below or ask a question! 

See you soon for the 2512 update!

entry.title: 4 Important enhancements related to Object type in SAP PM
entry.content:
Here are the top four important enhancements related to object type in SAP PM:

  1. Default value of authorization group: The object type can be proposed for motors, pumps, compressors, electrical distribution lines, transmission lines, assembly lines, etc. Now for each of the object types there can be a default value of authorization group. We can maintain those values in a Z table. If any new object type is introduced, a default value can be added. The user can add the respective value of the authorization group later on.
  2. Default value of Planner group: Planner groups generally work for a specific department or specific type of machine. In such a situation we can determine a specific planner group based on object type. This can be done by enhancing and maintaining the planner group and object type in the Z table.
  3. Determine the catalog profile based on the type of object type: The catalog profile is very important for adding technical findings in the form of codes in a notification. The catalog profile should be specific to the technical object or technical object type. So, you can maintain a catalog profile and object type in a Z table and determine the catalog profile automatically at the time of creating a technical object. At the time of creating a notification, the catalog profile is determined automatically as a part of the standard SAP feature.
  4. Determine the class and assign it to the equipment: You can determine the class automatically with respect to object type. For example, for object type "electrical pole," you can do an enhancement to determine its respective class automatically. You need to maintain the class and object type in a z table. It helps to assign the correct class to the technical object.

 

You can use user exit IEQM0003 for the check at the time of saving the equipment.

You can use the BAPI => BAPI_OBJCL_CHANGE to assign the class as per the object type in a equipment.

Regards,

Vaibhav Mishra

entry.title: Best Practice Implementation of the SAP Geographical Enablement Framework - Initial Setup
entry.content:
Introduction

The SAP Geographical Enablement Framework (in short SAP Geo Framework), as its name suggests, is a framework that is used to add geo spatial attributes to any S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition or S/4HANA on-Premise business object. This consists of configuration and ABAP class development for each object to be geo enabled. 

Because of the way an end user might wish to render an object on the map, and what business functions they wish to use from the map can differ greatly among customers and industries, SAP provides sample implementations of configuration and ABAP classes. This  provides out of the box working examples that can be evaluated, and used as templates for the customer implementation project. In many cases the delivered examples are not used in the productive environment, with a customer enhanced copy of the object used.

This blog series will be a technical discussion suited to consultants, and customer power users, involved in implementation of the Geo Framework. Reading and following the documentation is extremely important as the majority of implementation issues I have seen over the years are directly attributed to not following this first piece of advice. I will be focusing on the new S/4HANA 2025 release where we no longer require the installation of the SAP_GEF schema, and now have the spatial data storage in ABAP DDIC tables. Other than location of the spatial content, the majority of the information is also applicable for previous releases. Due to current technical constraints, query access to the spatial data is still via a native HANA view which now reside in the S/4HANA schema with a prefix '/1BCAMDP/GEF_' for easy identification.

The Geo Framework consists of 3 main components.

  1. An ABAP based persistence layer that is responsible for spatial relevant database access via ABAP ADBC calls. API's are part of this layer.
  2. A Map Provider implementation that consists of a web service and handles incoming requests and output processing. The default implementation from SAP is based on the Esri REST specification for a feature service. SAP only implements functions of the Esri REST specification required for internal use, but it may allow use by non-SAP tools with a customers own ICF node and subclass implementation, in conjunction with own method redefinitions in the Geo Object class. Other map API's could be implemented as an alternate or parallel Map Provider. Multiple map providers may be active at the same time. When a map provider API requires a license, the customer is responsible for any licensing agreements. The Geo Framework is a BYOL (Bring your own license) model since SAP relies on third party API's for comprehensive map provider visualization tools. SAP provides an Esri based map provider by default, given our large customer base overlap. If the SAP customer is already an Esri customer, then it is likely an agreement is already in place, but should be confirmed with Esri.  If the customer does NOT have a current Esri relationship and wishes to use the Geo Framework, they must engage with Esri themselves. There are a number of options for this, one being a subscription to Esri's ArcGIS Location Platform as described in the master note mentioned below.
  3. A Javascript based UI5 Map and Editor application for accessing spatially aware business data, external GIS data, and initiating business processes from the map. The map could be described as a context aware Fiori Launchpad where tiles are replaced by actions executed on the objects selected from the map. Essentially the map fills parameters to allow direct access to the business application, bypassing the typical entry screens.

Installation

The first step in implementation is to understand the components required for correct implementation. Reading  Master Note 3149622 should be one of the first tasks for the implementor. This note will explain the backend and UI components for each release on S/4HANA, which releases support which version of the Esri API the map uses, 3.x or 4.x, and which catalogs to use in role creation. It also provides information on SAP Web Dispatcher port mapping should there be a Central UI Server deployment. Customers running S/4HANA 2022 or later should use the 4.x API applications where possible. Esri has deprecated the 3.x API and SAP will discontinue supporting this version of the map application in a future release. If for some reason the 3.x API is required, it is possible to run both applications in parallel during a cutover period.

Enterprise search is utilized within the Geo Framework and needs to be configured as per the documentation.

It is strongly suggested that all outstanding notes released after your current support package be applied for the Geo Framework UI and backend systems. Component CA-EPT-GEF is used for the core framework and UI. Other applications such as Enterprise Asset Management have their own component for application related notes, PM-GEF in the case of spatial asset management.

I always suggest that before any major decisions are made that the sample configuration for the Business Partner be activated and tested. This ensures that the underlying framework is configured and running before you start activating configuration for other business applications, or customers own objects. Simply activating another application component Business function without the default Sample implementation will fail, as the Application specific BC sets rely of the default implementation to be in place. For example, all the Framework specific fields such as the Object Key and Geometry Context that are in the Application configuration will not be present and the BC set will fail to install correctly.

Spatial reference 3857 will be active in the S/4HANA database by default so for now there is no need to worry about an own SRS as the delivered OpenStreetmap basemap uses SRS 3857.

The start to installation you need to activate the Business Function FND_GEF and associated BC set as per the documentation. Depending on the activating user authorizations, such as cross client config, the BC sets associated with the Business Function may not fully activate. Check the status in the Switch Framework Browser. Below you can see everything is active with the yellow highlighted items indicating only certain clients are active. Clicking on the traffic lights will show activation status for each client. 

Should you not have all green check marks, you can go to the switch, by double clicking the highlighted node, and manually activate the dependent BC sets as per image below.

Press the highlighted icon and you will get the popup. Press Continue and you get the following popup. Make the settings as per image and Continue.

This will manually rerun the BC set activation, overwriting previous entry ensuring activation in the current client.

Go back to the Switch Framework Browser to verify the activation status.

Review and adjustment of Customizing

After activation of the BC sets it is recommended you perform a quick review of the customizing, and make adjustments as required for your particular instance. More on this further down.

The first configuration to check is the Geo Framework Map Provider settings accessed using transaction code GEF_GIS_CONFIG.

You need to ensure that the ICF node for the GIS Service Path is active in the system.

Ensure that the Esri data types were correctly loaded. These data types will be used to set the data types of the ABAP fields being sent to the map.

Ensure that all Framework Technical Structure Fields are present. These fields are used within the framework to move geometry data back and forth between the database, ABAP programs and the map. The Map Specific Data type is important and an incorrect setting here can produce strange behavior.

The next node is an alternate view of the Geo Object field mappings from the transaction GEF_FRW_CONFIG which allows you to view all Geo Objects configured in the client and mass process the field mappings, rather than individually in the Framework Configuration transaction. Here we see the two Geo objects for the person (PERSON) and Organization (ORGN) belonging to the Sample Business Object, Business Partner (BP) .

Field mappings for the PERSON Geo Object.

After verifying the Map Provider configuration, the geo enablement settings for the business data need to be verified with transaction GEF_FRW_CONFIG.

The following diagram shows the default configuration for a new S/4HANA 2025 system. Many sections are self explanatory and I will skip over unless there is an explicit change from previous releases. If you need more detail, please see the appropriate documentation page.

If you are upgrading from a system that was at some stage in the past an ECC system, your Schema Name in GDB may need to be adjusted. A new S/4HANA system will be installed with a database schema named SAPHANADB and is the default value from the BC set. ECC HANA database schemas typically used the format SAP<SID>, for example SAPEPR, and you need to adjust the configuration value to your system configuration.

The Maximum Record Count defines the number of records selected for each map tile query. Early versions of ArcGIS server (the default Geo Framework Map Provider is based on the Esri REST specification) by default limited the maximum records per query to 1000. Later releases increased this to 2000. SAP does not recommend exceeding the default Esri values for performance reasons. SImilar situation with the Enterprise Search Max Count values.

For those familiar with the pre 2025 versions of the Geo Framework, you will notice the new ABAP DDIC based database class and the upper case prefix and view name. The prefix when using DDIC tables is limited to a maximum of 13 characters to fit with the DDIC table name format. The last 3 characters are used for the geometry type to be stored in the table giving 16 chars total (3 used by SAP). It is suggested to keep the view name short but meaningful with the default being GEOM.

The system settings level database fields are now only for backwards compatibility. From S/4HANA 2025, the Business Object will be the default level for the database settings, hence there are none of the new geometry dimension settings at the System Level.

For the Icons for Symbols setting you need to ensure the s4 version of the icons are configured. Those upgrading from the older UIGEF001 map component may be using the mime repository path for the old version which is not present in a 2025 system.

Each Business Object now has it's own number range object with a single number range interval '01', replacing the single native HANA sequence used for the GEF_OBJECTID value. Please see documentation for number range details. The Business partner uses a default number range object GEF_GEN which will be used as a fallback if a business object configuration does not contain a number range. When migrating from HANA spatial table to DDIC tables, the number range status for each number range needs to initially be set to a value greater than the current HANA sequence value.

The Geometry Attributes section mimic the database settings of an Esri featureclass, defining the dimensionality and any default values when working with Z coordinates. Enhancements to the documentation on Geometry Attributes will be part of the future FPS1 documentation.

*** Deviation from default configuration ***

Since the initial BC set development, SAP has moved to CDS based search helps and the customizing in S/4HANA 2025 FPS0 requires adjustment. Updated BC sets with more examples will be coming in a future feature pack release.

For now we can make some adjustments that will provide more insight into the workings.

1. Update the Geo object settings to use the CDS based Enterprise Search model. You will notice a Z class for the Implementation class. We will cover this lower down but include in the change now. Make the same changes for both the PERSON and ORGN Geo Objects. The Z class will be the same as default with Z prefix (ZCL_GEF_BP_PERSON_SAMPLE and ZCL_GEF_BP_ORGANIZATION_SAMPLE).

2. Update the PERSON and ORGN field mappings to use the CDS Enterprise Search Models. You need to add mappings from the structure fields to the search model fields. Update your configuration as per the diagram below for the PERSON and ORGN. This tells the search to use the search field names in place of the structure field names.

3. Now we need to create new Geo Object classes as a copy of the delivered class. You will need developer access for this task. I will detail the PERSON in class ZCL_GEF_BP_PERSON_SAMPLE but do the same for the ORGN in class ZCL_GEF_BP_ORGANIZATION_SAMPLE.

Update method IF_GEF_BO_SERVICE~COMPOSE_GEF_OBJKEY as below. This will allow use of the CDS field name in the determination of the GEF_OBJKEY value.

METHOD if_gef_bo_service~compose_gef_objkey.

    FIELD-SYMBOLS: <fs_bp_number> TYPE any.

    IF mv_has_cds_esh_search = abap_true.
      ASSIGN COMPONENT 'BUSINESSPARTNER' OF STRUCTURE is_bo_data TO <fs_bp_number>.
    ELSE.
      ASSIGN COMPONENT 'PARTNER' OF STRUCTURE is_bo_data TO <fs_bp_number>.
    ENDIF.

    rv_gef_objkey = <fs_bp_number>.

  ENDMETHOD.

Create a redefinition of method IF_GEF_BO_SERVICE~INITIALIZE_ENTERPRISE_SEARCH. This will use the mapped field names in the search.

  METHOD if_gef_bo_service~initialize_enterprise_search.

    DATA: lt_filter    TYPE geft_geo_filter,
          ls_filter    TYPE gefs_geo_filter,
          lv_flt_field TYPE typename,
          ls_selopt    TYPE rsdsselopt,
          ls_selcrit   TYPE /plmb/s_spi_selection_param,
          lt_sel_crit  TYPE /plmb/t_spi_selection_param.

    " call super initialize
    CALL METHOD super->if_gef_bo_service~initialize_enterprise_search
      EXPORTING
        io_req_context = io_req_context.

    lt_filter = io_req_context->get_geo_filters( ).

* convert geo filter table into select criteria table
    cl_eams_gef_util=>build_selection_criteria(
      EXPORTING
        it_filter             = lt_filter
      IMPORTING
        et_selcrit            = lt_sel_crit
    ).

*-------------------------------- SAP Note 2831610 ------------------------------------*
* mapping customizing structure to Enterprise Search format
    IF  mv_has_cds_esh_search = abap_true
     OR abap_true = cl_esh_cdsabap_tools=>is_cds_connector( iv_connector_id = io_req_context->get_connector_id( ) iv_use_runtime_load = abap_false ).
      mv_has_cds_esh_search = abap_true.
      cl_eams_gef_util=>map_selection_criteria(
        EXPORTING
          is_mapping_dictionary = sc_bp_cds_esh_mapping_dict
        CHANGING
          ct_select_criteria    = lt_sel_crit ).
    ENDIF.

    cl_eams_gef_util=>add_filter_to_search(
      EXPORTING
        io_req_context    =  io_req_context   " Request Context for Enterprise Search: for Application
        it_sel_crit       =  lt_sel_crit   " Selection Criteria for Queries
        io_search_request =  mo_request       " Search Request
    ).
*    CATCH cx_gef_exception.    " (

  ENDMETHOD.

The remaining settings for the Business Partner Geo objects are described in the documentation and should be functional without adjustment.

The final configuration defines what is visible on the map for the user. This is managed via profiles and configured in transaction GEF_UI_CONFIG where you customize the map user interface. Please see the documentation for detail but I will provide brief explanation of the settings below.

It is suggested you add a server setting pointing to your UI system. This can be used to ensure the correct path for mime repository map icons is used, especially if deployed with a central UI server. In the above graphic there are servers for alternate basemaps, GIS servers and portals, but this is not required for the base configuration and will be discussed in later blogs.

The default basemap delivered is OpenStreetMap which requires no server settings, hence no entries delivered by default.

The basemap set includes the initial map location and zoom level on launch. You can adjust these values to a location more appropriate for your organization.

In the Icons for Symbols entry from above, I have enhanced with a server pointing to my base system as this is configured with embedded Fiori.

The default configuration comes with no Reference Layers configured. The default Business layer group contains the business layers for PERSON and ORGN geo objects. I will show this on the map menu.

In order to search for a location you need to configure a map locator service such as:

This configuration should now allow the default use of the map after allocation of appropriate authorizations.

Authorizations and roles

In order to use the map applications, the required authorizations must be configured as defined in the documentation. A role is required with Authorization Object G_GEF_GEOM to control access to geometry and is created via the template SAP_GEF_USR. Authorizations are also required for users with the task to create new spatial tables and views. I will cover these topics more in a future part of the series. 

There is no standard role at this time for the Geo Framework apps.  The catalogs required are described in the aforementioned Master Note (see the importance of reading this note 😉 ). It is suggested you create a role for the map/editor applications and another for the authorizations. There are no authorizations directly on the map applications, it is the content of the map that is controlled. Since different business objects may have different access requirements, there may be numerous geometry roles in addition to the role containing the map applications. You may also choose to create a role for the map and a role for the editor as a means of controlling edit access on top of geometry level control.

Now that we have configuration and authorizations setup we can test the map as per the Application Functions section of the documentation.

Let's explore the map

Here my user has roles for both the 3.x API apps (Spatial Asset Management) and the 4.x apps (Spatial Data Management). The Explorer app will verify configuration for displaying map layers and the Editor app will verify that the Enterprise Search is correctly configured. The Editor can select ANY Geo object that meets the geo object filter requirement. To do this, it does an Enterprise search with the field filter values in the selection parameters. The result list then shows which objects do and do not have geometry with a geometry indicator. From there you open the map at the default extent defined in various ways. This will be covered in another part of the series. The Explorer can only display an object that already has geometry.

Clicking on the Spatial Data Editor and searching for a Person we get the list of active Person business partners in the system. Select one and it will take you to the editor to add geometry. Select the point and click and save. Do this for a few items.

Then search for an Organization and edit geometry. For the Organization you will notice a context menu as this Geo Object has multiple contexts allowed. Select a context and create a geometry. Notice how the selected context sets the allowed geometry editing tools. After creating different geometry contexts, select the all contexts and see the overview.

Now we can display on the Explorer map and execute business transactions if configured. There are none in the BP sample but we will see this in the next article of the series.

Clicking on the Spatial Data Explorer we load the map at the default location defined in the Basemap set used in the default profile.

In this image we have the Business Partner layers shown in the menu. Note, the system I am using also has EAM configured so please ignore this for now. We will cover this in the next article of the series.

In this image we see the PERSON's on the map.

Click on the person and you will see the popup.

Congratulations. Once you have the ability to add geometry to an object, display the object in business layers, and see data in the popup, your system is ready for further customizing and additional Business Object implementations.

I hope that this first article was helpful in getting the base SAP Geo Framework up and running, and peak your interest in executing your S/4HANA applications from a map.

In the next part of the series we will cover the activation of Enterprise Asset Management Business Objects.

entry.title: How to Connect a OPC-UA Device to SAP Asset Performance Management
entry.content:
Introduction

New month, new big release! With version 2511 of SAP Asset Performance Management (APM), the Embedded IoT capabilities powered by Cumulocity reached General Availability (GA) status. In this post from the Asset Performance Management product team, you can get an overview of the 4 IoT applications that are now available to SAP Asset Performance Management customers.

To celebrate this next milestone, we (the topic experts from Cumulocity) wanted to share our next guide with you as a follow-up on our post where we showed how you can easily connect your smartphone to get started quickly.

Overview

To get started with SAP Asset Performance Management and connect real-world machines, you can leverage existing device protocols like OPC-UA. In this guide, we demonstrate how to use the Embedded IoT platform (powered by Cumulocity) to connect physical devices via OPC-UA and correlate them with assets provided by SAP Asset Performance Management.

We will leverage multiple components in this scenario:

  • Simulated pumps with an OPC-UA client
  • Thin-edge.io running an OPC-UA Server and the Cumulocity OPC-UA Gateway to transfer the data to Cumulocity
  • Cumulocity as an embedded IoT cloud system and capabilities like
    • Device Management
      • OPC-UA Remote Management
      • Software Management
      • Cloud OT Protocol Mapping using Device Protocols

    • Cockpit
      • Dashboarding

    • Digital Twin Manager
      • Mapping & Correlating Device data to Asset data in SAP Asset Performance Management

  • SAP Asset Performance Management
    • Asset Data Management
    • Maintenance Rules & Alert Management

Let’s get started!

Prerequisites

To follow this guide, the following prerequisites must be fulfilled:

  • You have access to an SAP Asset Performance Management instance including Embedded IoT platform.
  • You have Docker and Docker Compose installed on your machine. We will use them to simulate a device, including an OPC-UA simulation server.

Step-by-Step Guide

1. Start thin-edge.io demo container

To simulate a device that runs an OPC-UA + simulation server, we leverage thin-edge.io, which is a powerful open-source edge framework that can connect with ease to the embedded IoT platform Cumulocity. For demonstration purposes, there is a demo container available that installs thin-edge with tons of pre-installed and configured plugins that let us manage the device fully remotely, showcasing the power of the Cumulocity platform.

First of all, you need to download a docker compose file:

curl -LSs https://raw.githubusercontent.com/thin-edge/tedge-demo-container/main/demos/docker-compose/device/docker-compose.yaml > docker-compose.yaml

Or using wget:

wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/thin-edge/tedge-demo-container/main/demos/docker-compose/device/docker-compose.yaml

Before we start the container, we need to register it in Cumulocity. From within SAP Asset Performance Management launchpad, navigate to the “Manage Devices” application to sign-in to the Embedded IoT platform.

Within “Device Management” navigate to “Management” → “Trusted Certificates”. Here click on “Add CA Certificate” in the top right toolbar which will add the CA certificate to the tenant which is used to issue new device certificates. Please note: If you already have the CA certificate added, the button “Add CA certificate” is disabled and you can proceed.

Navigate to “Devices” -> “Registration”. Please remember & save your tenant URL from your browser address bar. You need to provide it in some steps below, e.g.:

https://<Your Cumulocity Tenant URL, ending with *.iot.sap>/apps/devicemanagement/index.html#/deviceregistration

Click on “Register device” → “General”.

Toggle to create device certificate during device registration and enter the following:

  • Device ID: ThinEdge-cooling-line3
  • One-time password: in4mU32icNVjj3c
    (Important: This password here is just for demonstration purposes. Replace this with any secret token of your choice)

Click on “Next” to create the device registration.

Next you can start the demo container with the following command in the folder where you have downloaded the docker-compose.yaml to:

docker compose up -d

Afterwards we need to bootstrap it with the following commands (take the domain for the saved tenant URL).

docker compose exec tedge tedge config set c8y.url <Your Cumulocity Tenant domain without https://>

And (replace the one-time password with your selected password)

docker compose exec tedge tedge cert download c8y --device-id ThinEdge-cooling-line3 --one-time-password in4mU32icNVjj3c

Finally we need to connect thin-edge:

docker compose exec tedge tedge connect c8y

 

In the „Device Management“ application, you should now see the thin-edge device with the Name “ThinEdge-cooling-line-3”.

2. Make OPC-UA Gateway & OPC-UA Simulation Server available via Software Repository

Great, thin-edge devices are up and running! As a next step, we want to create a Cumulocity OPC-UA gateway within the demo container that allows us to establish a connection to an OPC-UA server.

Cumulocity in combination with thin-edge allows to remotely deploy and run Docker containers directly from the Cumulocity Device Management application in your browser. Therefore, there is no need to go to any console, do a Docker pull or run; it can be all done within the Device Management App of Cumulocity. To do this, we need to create so-called software artifacts, which contain a docker-compose.yml file.

Luckily, there is another thin-edge repository that does the job for us: https://github.com/thin-edge/opcua-device-gateway-container

For our purpose, we can use the following Docker Compose file:

https://raw.githubusercontent.com/thin-edge/opcua-solution-blueprint/refs/heads/main/software/docker-compose-opcua-device-gateway-demo-container.yml

Within SAP Asset Performance Management, navigate to the “Manage Devices” application -> Management -> Software Repository.

Click on “Add Software” and provide the following information. Select “Upload File” and select the docker-compose.yml file you created in the previous step.

  • Software: OPC-UA Gateway
  • Description: Container to run OPC-UA Gateway within tedge demo container
  • Device type filter: thin-edge.io
  • Software type: container-group
  • Version: 1.0.0
