  • Provide a file path: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/thin-edge/opcua-solution-blueprint/refs/heads/main/software/docker-compose-opcua-device-gateway-demo-container.yml

 

Repeat the step for adding an OPC-Server that simulates data but using the following Docker Compose file and details:

  • Software: OPC-UA Demo Server
  • Description: OPC-UA Demo Server that simulates device data
  • Device type filter: thin-edge.io
  • Software type: container-group
  • Version: 1.0.0
  • Provide a file path: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/thin-edge/opcua-solution-blueprint/refs/heads/main/software/docker-compose-opcua-demo-server.yml

In the end, you should have two software entries added in the Software Repository:

 

3. Install Cumulocity OPC-UA Gateway & OPC-UA Simulation Server

With the software artifacts for the Cumulocity OPC-UA Gateway and the OPC-UA Simulation Server created successfully, we want to install them in our thin-edge demo container by using the Device Management capabilities.

Within “Manage Devices”, navigate to “Devices” -> “All devices” and search for “ThinEdge-cooling-line3,” which should return our thin-edge demo container.

When clicking on the device, we see all the capabilities. One capability of the thin-edge demo container is to install software remotely via the “Software” Tab. At the bottom, click on “Install software.” In the opened windows, you should see the opc-ua gateway & opc-ua simulator. Select for both the version “1.0.0” and click on “Install.”

Finally, start the installation process by clicking on “Apply changes.”After a short time, you should see that both components were successfully installed.You can confirm success by selecting the “Services” Tab. The opc-ua demo server and opc-ua gateway should be listed there.

4. Configure the OPC-UA Server

Now that both components are installed and running in our thin-edge demo container, we need to configure them. Continuing on the “ThinEdge-cooling-line3” device, navigate to “Child devices.” You should see a new child device named “OPCUAGateway”, please select it.

There, you can find a Tab called “OPC UA server.” Click on “Add server” and provide the following information:

  • Server name: OPC-UA Demo Server
  • Server URL: opc.tcp://opcserver:4840

Keep all other settings to default and “Save” the server.

With the connection to the server configured, the “Child Devices” Tab now shows an established server connection that appears as another child device called “OPC-UA Demo Server”. Click on it and select “Address space” to check the simulated and provided objects and sensors. Please note that it will take a few minutes for the address space scan to complete. The “Pump01” is especially interesting as it will be used in the next step.

5. Create Device Protocol

With the simulated pump connected via OPC-UA we can map data from the pump object to the Cumulocity domain model. We will do that by creating a so-called “Device Protocol”

From SAP Asset Performance Management, navigate again to the “Manage Devices” application which takes you seamlessly to the “Device Management” capability in Cumulocity. The device protocols can be defined in “Device Types” -> “Device Protocols”. Click on “Add device protocol”, select “OPC UA” and provide the following information:

  • Name: Pump01
  • Description: Device Type for Pump01
  • Reference server: OPC-UA Demo Server
  • Select “Root” node

When clicking on “Create” you can configure the variables. Click on “Add variable”. Navigate to Pump01 and select “flow”.Toggle measurement with the following settings:

  • Type: flow
  • Series: flow
  • Unit: l/m

Click “Save”.

Repeat the steps for “bearingTemperature”:

  • Type: bearingTemperature
  • Series: bearingTemperature
  • Unit: C

And finally “power”:

  • Type: power
  • Series: power
  • Unit: W

If you want you can also create the other variables like described in the screenshot below:

In the “Data Reporting” section select “Cyclic read” with a reading interval of “3000” msec. Finally activate (on top) and save the Device Protocol.

Navigate back to “Devices”. You should see a new Device there called “Pump01” that represents the created protocol definition. When clicking on “Measurements” and activating real-time by clicking on the dot in the top you should see the values coming in.

6. Creating Indicators in SAP Asset Performance Management

With the data available in Cumulocity you can now focus on assigning it to technical objects from SAP Asset Performance Management. This step bridges the OT and IT world, bringing device semantics into the process and master data context from the IT world. In our case, we assign the simulated pump to a "Cooling Water Circulation Pump" but you can assign it to any other asset of your choice as available in your system. To proceed with assigning the sensors, you need a representation of them as indicators in SAP Asset Performance Management. Follow thedocumentation to create three indicators:

  • Pump Bearing Temperature
  • Pump Flow Rate
  • Motor Power

7. Assign Data Points to Indicators

SAP Asset Performance Management automatically provisions Technical Objects along with their indicators into the Embedded IoT platform for data contextualization. From the Asset Performance Management launchpad, open the “Map Devices to Assets” application, which navigates to the “Digital Twin Manager” within Cumulocity.

Use the search in the top right area to search for the identifier of your Technical Object (e.g. 217100093 from the screenshot above, or any parent object such as 1710) to easily open the corresponding asset. The indicators are shown with their nomenclature from SAP Asset Performance Management and can now be “linked” to their corresponding measurements/series from the OPC UA protocol definition via the “Link source” button.

After successful assignment, the measurement data will be made available within SAP Asset Performance Management.

8. Review data in SAP Asset Performance Management

You can validate that time series data from your simulation server is now available on the Technical Object for further processing within SAP Asset Performance Management. Open the “Explore Technical Objects” application and navigate to the “Cooling Water Circulation Pump”. On the “Indicator Monitoring” tab, you can see the incoming data in the time series chart.

Please note that it can take up to 90 minutes until data is available in Asset Performance Management.

Conclusion & Next Steps

This guide demonstrated how you can connect an industrial device via OPC-UA to Embedded IoT powered by Cumulocity and assign its measurement data to an existing technical object in SAP Asset Performance Management.

This described setup uses a simulated pump. If you want to leverage OPC-UA in a productive use case, you may follow the steps described here: https://github.com/thin-edge/opcua-solution-blueprint?tab=readme-ov-file#production-like-deployment-examples

Doing so you would not use the thin-edge demo container, but install thin-edge.io locally on a gateway that has access to an already available OPC-UA server where devices are already connected to, or utilizing a certified device from Cumulocity’s extensive hardware partner catalog.

entry.title: Extending SAP Asset Performance Management: Three Approaches to Tailoring Your Asset Operations
entry.content:
In asset management, no two organizations operate the same way. While standard software provides a robust foundation, the ability to adapt to industry-specific regulations and distinct business processes is where you find your true efficiency.

At SAP, we understand this need for flexibility. That's why SAP Asset Performance Management (APM) is built on a modern, cloud-native architecture designed to be extended. Whether you are a customer, partner, or consultant, this blog highlights how you can leverage APM’s extensibility options to build tailored, value-added solutions.

 

Why Extensibility Matters

Extensibility is not just a technical feature - it's what makes APM work the way your business actually runs. By extending the solution, you can:

  • Address industry-specific needs by closing functional gaps that matter in your daily operations.
  • Enhance user productivity with role-focused UIs that cut out unnecessary clicks and complexities.
  • Integrate seamlessly with your existing IT landscape without disrupting the core application.

Here are the three main ways you can customize APM. The first two methods are also collectively known as SaaS Extensibility.

1. Extending Data and Services (OData APIs)

The foundation of any custom solution is data. APM provides robust OData APIs that let you add new data fields without modifying the core structure.

What You Can Do

A. Add custom fields and custom entities

Need to track "Compliance Due Date" or the "Safety Criticality Level" on a specific recommendation? You can extend existing entities with additional fields or create entirely new entities to capture this critical information, without changing the standard application model.

  • Custom entities are customer-defined data structures that allow organizations to store and manage business information beyond the standard application model.
  • Custom fields are customer-defined attributes added to an existing entities to capture additional business-specific information.

B. Build custom applications

Develop custom applications using extended services (APIs) to introduce new business logic or functionalities that complement APM.

=> Learn how to extend ODATA APIs in CAP: Extending the Data Model

2. Customizing the User Interface (UI)

Once you have the right data, you need to present it effectively. APM offers robust options for extending SAP Fiori-based applications to match your workflow.

What You Can Do:

  • Show Your Custom Fields: Make your custom data (like that "Compliance Due Date") visible as a new column in lists like Manage Recommendations or as a new field in details pages.
  • Organize Information: Group related information together. For example, you could create a specific "Safety Assessment and Financial Analysis" tab on the Recommendation page to put all risk mitigation and cost benefit details in one easy to find spot.
  • Smart Controls: Change how data looks and control how fields are rendered – like changing a text input to a dropdown list for consistency or reordering fields to prioritize key data.

=> Learn how to extend UI5 Fiori based applications: Extending UI Annotations

Example 1: Recommendation Object Page showing new custom fields for Version tracking in the Header and Information section.

Example 2: Recommendation Object Page showing a custom 'Attachments' tab with external links and sensitivity details

Example 3: Recommendation Object Page showing new custom fields for ‘Integrity Critical’ and ‘Run-to-Fail' status

 

Example 4: Recommendation List Page showing new columns and filters for custom fields - Version, Integrity Criticality, Run to Fail status

 

3. Adapting for Different Users

Different people need different things. APM leverages the SAPUI5 Extensibility framework, offering three levels of customization so everyone has the right view.

A. End User Personalization ("My View")

  • Who: Individual Users

  • What: Users can save their own filter settings as "Variants" (e.g., "Critical Rotor Failures Q3"), add shortcuts to objects you use often, and move applications around on the Fiori Launchpad.

  • Why: Increases individual efficiency without requiring IT support.

=> Learn more about SAPUI5 Flexibility for End Users: End User Personalization

B. Key User Adaptation ("Our View")

  • Who: Business Experts / Key Users
  • What: Using intuitive, no-code tools, key users can adapt the UI for entire teams. This includes hiding irrelevant fields, renaming labels to match internal terminology, or creating shared views for specific roles (e.g., an "Operations View" vs. a "Reliability View").
  • Why: Tailors the application for specific teams without writing custom code.

=> Learn more about SAPUI5 Flexibility for Key Users: Key User Adaptation

=> How to Begin with Key User Adaptation: Adapting SAP Fiori UIs at Runtime

C. Developer Adaptation ("Custom Code")

  • Who: Professional Developers

  • What: For complex requirements, developers can use SAP Business Application Studio to inject custom UI components, integrate external data services, or implement complex logic.

  • Why: Solves unique, complex business challenges while keeping the core app safe and updatable.

Note: Developer Adaptation is currently not supported in APM.

 

Getting Started

We want to make extending APM as easy as possible. To help you start quickly, we provide:

  • Template Projects: Downloadable SAP CAP projects pre-configured for extending specific APM applications. These include the basic code and test data to speed up development.
  • Step-by-Step Guides: Step-by-step documentation covering everything from setting up your test tenant to deploying and maintaining your extensions.

Best Practices for Success

To ensure your extensions are robust and future-proof, keep these principles in mind:

  • Don't Break the Core: Always use the provided frameworks (OData extensions, UI Adaptation) rather than modifying core code.
  • Focus on Value: Focus on extensions that address a clear business need and add tangible value to your users.
  • Design for Stability: Write clean, modular and secure code that is easy to maintain and scale.
  • Keep it Secure: Adhere to SAP security guidelines for data access and authorization.
  • Performance Aware: Make sure you extensions don’t slow down the core application, especially if you are working with large datasets.
  • Test Rigorously: Ensure your extensions function correctly under various scenarios.

Conclusion

Extensibility is the key to getting the most out of your asset management strategy. By leveraging these tools, you can transform APM into a solution that fits your business perfectly - driving greater efficiency, intelligence, and operational excellence.

Ready to get started? Check out the official documentation and our architectural guidelines to begin tailoring APM to your unique needs today.

SaaS Extensibility for SAP Asset Performance Management

Extensibility Options for Recommendations

entry.title: SAP JOULE & ME : My First JOULE Skill in SAP EHSM-Incident Management Without Coding
entry.content:
This blog outlines the SAP JOULE Skill setup for the creation of an EHS Incident . I have leveraged SAP JOULE`s low-code  and no-code capabilities to build something impactful.

Fact Sheet: In SAP`s definition, JOULE is not an acronym. It is the name of SAP`s generative AI assistant. SAP named its digital assistant after "James Prescott Joule", who studied the nature of heat and discovered its relationship to mechanical work. The SI unit of energy, the Joule (J) is named after him.

SAP Joule welcome screen.

 

What is SAP Joule:

SAP Joule is an AI copilot that is grounded in organization`s business data. It aims to understand our requests, retrieve information, and complete our tasks using natural language in a conversational way.

  • Joule Skill :  It is a predefined capability that executes a specific task within the business context.
  • Joule Agent: It is a more advanced AI powered assistant that uses multiple JOULE Skills and other agents to complete complex tasks/workflows.

(In my view, JOULE Skill is like a hand that executes what it is told, and JOULE Agent is like a brain that decides which skill to be used, in what order, and under what conditions. )

SAP Joule supports four main capability types, viz: Informational, navigational, transactional, and analytical.

Disclaimer:  This blog outlines the "Transactional"  capability type and provides a high-level overview of Joule Skill setup. It is not intended to cover the full technical details or advanced configurations of the SAP Joule Skill.

Let us first understand SAP Joule capability types...

  • Informational: An Informational capability (conversational search) helps users retrieve necessary information from SAP Help portal or documents uploaded to the joule service.

 

  • Navigational: A navigational capability helps users find and navigate to the functionality or apps they are looking for without having to go through multiple links to reach a destination. Joule supports navigation to selected core SAP Fiori apps in the Sourcing and Procurement, Finance, Sales, cross-area capabilities (manage Business Partners, My Inbox, etc.), Service Management, Enterprise Portfolio and Project Management. (Source : Finding Apps with SAP Joule Navigational Capability )

Note: A navigational use case may be viewed as a streamlined version of a transactional use case.

  • Transactional: A transactional capability helps users with their day to day tasks. Using transactional capability, joule helps users execute specific business tasks or actions.

 

  • Analytical: An analytical Insights capability helps users explore their data and gain insights into their business, in a natural, conversational experience. It Performs deeper analysis, often combining data sources. (Note: The Joule Skill analytical images are sourced from the SAP Business AI website). Analytical insights are powered by SAP Analytics Cloud.

 

My Business Use Case:

In day-to-day operations, EHS incidents are critical, and We have a policy in place that allows all our employees across the locations to report EHS Incidents (Near Miss, Safety Observation, Incident) based on what they observe at the location. With Joule skill, our users can report incident instantly using a conversational interface, and minimize potential risks to the organization. Once EHS incident is reported, our Incident Manager is notified and it is addressed promptly.

Let us dive into the details...

First things first, let us verify the OData services available for EHS Incident in S/4HANA system. To do this, log in to the SAP API hub and review the EHS Incident OData Service schema to understand the available fields.

Now let us activate EHS Incident OData service in S/4HANA system.

After activating the EHS Incident OData service, we create Action Project. We use action project for creating and display the EHS Incidents via an OData service representing an SAP System.

To do so, choose OData Destinations and create an action project "EHS_Incident_Management".

Now choose required Input and Output fields for this action.

For instance, I have chosen "Incident Title", "Incident Category", "Incident Date and Time" and "Incident Location Description" fields as Input. 

and for output, I have chosen "Incident ID",  "Incident Status",  "Incident Title", "Incident Category", "Incident Date and Time" and "Incident Location Description" fields.

Now let us first test the Action "Create an Incident".

EHS Incident has been successfully created in SAP S/4HANA System.

Now let us save the action project, release and publish.

Now let us create Joule skill. The lobby is a central page for creating, accessing, and managing our projects in SAP Build.

Enter the name and description of the Project.

Now let us create the JOULE SKILL. First things first, we must add the action that was created in earlier step for creating an EHS Incident under "Dependencies" tab.

after adding the dependencies for the EHS Incident Creation, we can now create Joule skill.

The description is very important because Joule will use it to identify the correct skill to select during the conversation.

 

Now let us define the Input and Output parameters for the Joule Skill.

 

For Instance, I have turned off the toggle switch "Allow Joule to generate a response" and configured my own conversation starter to make it easier for my users.

Now let us configure Input fields.

We have configured the parameters. Now let us add the action "Create Incident" that was created.

and we must choose the destination variable for the action.

Now let us map the Input fields using skill content.

Now let us configure the output message that will be displayed after the JOULE skill creates an EHS Incident.

If there are any errors in the Joule skill setup, the system displays them under the "Design Console" tab.

As there are no errors in the EHS Incident Joule Skill setup, we can now Save, Release and Deploy the joule skill.

We can maintain the "Release Notes" to ensure more transparency and avoid ambiguity across Joule Skill versions.

Now let us "Deploy" the Joule Skill.

Now, let us engage with SAP Joule to create an EHS Incident. To do this, we can access the respective environment under "Control Tower" and launch "Joule".

Now, we can either click on the button "Create EHS Incident" or have a conversation with joule to create an EHS Incident. For Instance, let us use the button "Create EHS Incident".

we can now provide the input details

as per our input details, SAP Joule Skill creates an EHS Incident with number "24".

let us check the Incident Number "24" in S4HANA System.

 

Let us ask Joule to provide all the EHS Incidents reported as of today.

 

😊With SAP Joule, I was able to setup my first SAP Joule Skill in SAP EHSM-Incident Management using OData Service without coding. It proves that anyone can streamline processes and add value with ease. It was truly transformative.

Stay tuned for more updates on SAP Joule Skills in Asset Management Space...

--

Yours Sincerely,

Pardhasaradhi Reddy. Chelikam

entry.title: Fixing Warranty Inheritance Issues in SAP PM: A Custom ABAP Report
entry.content:
In SAP Plant Maintenance (PM), warranty periods for technical objects are designed to be inherited automatically from superior functional locations to their subordinate locations. This is part of the standard functionality. However, in practice, this inheritance sometimes fails... leading to inconsistencies and manual corrections that consume time and create frustration for PM administrators.

The Problem

Although SAP provides automatic inheritance for warranty start and end dates, there are cases where this process does not work as expected. Common reasons include:

  • Data inconsistencies in the hierarchy.
  • Missing or incorrect object numbers.
  • Technical issues during creation or update of functional locations.

When inheritance fails, subordinate locations remain without warranty data, which can impact maintenance planning and compliance.

The Solution

Instead of relying solely on the standard process, we developed a lightweight ABAP report that ensures warranty data consistency across the hierarchy. The report reads warranty start and end dates from the parent functional location and applies them to all subordinate locations, writing the data directly into the BGMKOBJ table.

Key Features

  • Input: Superior functional location.
  • Logic: Reads warranty data from the parent and applies it to all child locations.
  • Execution: Uses MODIFY on BGMKOBJ, which automatically decides whether to update existing records or insert new ones.
  • Modes: Test mode for simulation and productive mode for actual updates.

Core Logic

LOOP AT lt_child ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<child>).
  IF p_test = abap_true.
    lv_status = |SIMULATION: Location { <child>-tplnr } would be updated or newly created.|.
  ELSE.
    CLEAR wa_bgmkobj_child.
    wa_bgmkobj_child = wa_bgmkobj_parent.
    wa_bgmkobj_child-j_objnr = <child>-objnr.
    wa_bgmkobj_child-erdat   = sy-datum.
    wa_bgmkobj_child-erzei   = sy-uzeit.
    wa_bgmkobj_child-gaerb   = 'X'.

    " MODIFY automatically decides whether to INSERT or UPDATE
    MODIFY bgmkobj FROM _bgmkobj_child.

    IF sy-subrc = 0.
      lv_status = |Location { <child>-tplnr } successfully processed.|.
    ELSE.
      lv_status = |Error processing location { <child>-tplnr }.|.
    ENDIF.
  ENDIF.

  WRITE: / lv_status.
ENDLOOP.

entry.title: SAP Joule Skill for Technical Completion of Maintenance Orders in S/4HANA Asset Management
entry.content:
This blog provides a brief overview of certain advanced functionalities, namely multiple action projects and if-condition expression, within a single JOULE Skill.

What is SAP Joule:

SAP Joule is an AI copilot that is grounded in organization`s business data. It aims to understand our requests, retrieve information, and complete our tasks using natural language in a conversational way.

  • Joule Skill :  It is a predefined capability that executes a specific task within the business context.
  • Joule Agent: It is a more advanced AI powered assistant that uses multiple JOULE Skills and other agents to complete complex tasks/workflows

If you would like a high-level overview of SAP JOULE , and how to setup an SAP JOULE skill using OData services, check out my latest blog post "SAP JOULE & ME : My First JOULE Skill in SAP EHSM-Incident Management Without Coding" by accessing the link below. This blog outlines the basic functionality of the JOULE skill used to create an EHS Incident in the S/4HANA system.

SAP JOULE & ME : My First JOULE Skill in SAP EHSM-Incident Management Without Coding 

My Business Use Case: TECO Maintenance Order with SAP Joule Skill.

In my maintenance procedure, a maintenance order can only be "Technically Closed (TECO)" if the system status contains both REL (Released) & CNF (Confirmed). This ensures that no order is prematurely closed without properly released and confirmed.

To achieve this functionality, I have designed an SAP JOULE skill that leverages:

  • Two Action Projects: This is to get the Maintenance Order Details and Performs Technical Closure.
  • An If Condition: This is to validate if the provided maintenance order has both REL & CNF statuses before allowing the TECO.

This approach enforces strict control mechanism and ensuring that the only valid maintenance orders reach the TECO stage.

Let us break it down step by step to see how this SAP JOULE skill performs the TECO for the maintenance order.

What is an Action Project:

Actions are used in SAP Build Process Automation to connect processes with external systems, be it SAP or Non-SAP system, and to make GET, POST, PATCH calls. The actions that are created here can be consumed directly in a JOULE Skill created in SAP JOULE Studio.

For instance, I have chosen OData V2 API : API_MAINTENANCEORDER for get Maintenance Order details and perform Technical Closure actions.

Now let us test the OData in the action project : EAM_Maintenance_Order_Management.

 

Mini Note: We must follow API guidelines when providing input values. We had better read the business documentation provided under the "Documents" tab in SAP API HUB.

First let us test the GET call : Get Maintenance Order Details by Maintenance Order Number.

Now let us test POST call :  Set Maintenance Order to Technically Completed. 

The API response was successful and status has been updated to TECO.

Once we release and publish the Action project, we can now create JOULE skill in SAP Build Lobby.

Let us setup Input Parameters for "TECO Maintenance Order" Joule skill. This field is used during the conversation with JOULE for TECO of the maintenance order.

As per our business use case, using this input value, i.e., Maintenance Order, we should first get maintenance order status details.

Let us use action project "Get entity from MaintenanceOrder by key" and map the values from the Joule skill input parameters to the action project input value.

If the Maintenance Order Status does not include the system statuses REL (Released) and CNF (Confirmed). the system should display an error message or else It should use the action "Invoke action SetMaintOrdToTechCompleted"

If the condition is satisfied, the system should execute the action project "Invoke action SetMaintOrdToTechCompleted".

As per SAP API documentation, the maintenance order must be enclosed in single quotes when we use POST call "Invoke action SetMaintOrdToTechCompleted".

To achieve this, I have maintained the expression to let the system automatically enclose the provided maintenance order number in singe quotes.

The expression for the condition to close the assigned notification is "true", and for the Maintenance Order, the expression should be as follows.

Now let us configure the "Error Message" when the maintenance order does not satisfy the condition.

For Instance, I have chosen message type as "Illustrated Message".

Now let us configure the "Successful Message" when the maintenance order satisfies the condition.

We can now "Release" and "Deploy" the JOULE skill in respective environment. Once we deploy it, our maintenance team can now interact with JOULE skill in natural language to "Technically Close" the maintenance order.

Let us now interact with JOULE to technically close the maintenance order.

Condition-1: If the system status of the maintenance order does not include the statuses "REL (Released)" & "CNF (Confirmed)", the JOULE skill should provide an error message.

Condition-2: If the system status of the maintenance order includes the statuses "REL (Released)" & "CNF (Confirmed)", the JOULE skill should provide a successful message.

😊This brings me to the end of the SAP JOULE skill set up for Technical Completion of the Maintenance Order. Stay tuned for more updates on SAP JOULE skills in Asset Management Space...

Key takeaway:

By combining two action projects with an If condition in a single SAP JOULE skill, we can build more flexible automations that respond to business logic in real time. 

 

--

Yours Sincerely,

Pardhasaradhi Reddy. Chelikam

entry.title: What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2601
entry.content:
January’s release of SAP Asset Performance Management introduces updates across industrial systems, reliability assessments, asset health monitoring, and performance analysis. Highlights include bulk activation and deactivation of system elements, more flexible risk and criticality assessment outcomes, CAP-based reliability assessment applications with extensibility, configurable RCM consequence questionnaires, validity periods for assessments, time-window–based rule processing, and embedded analytics dashboards.

Before we start here's a webcast tip!  Do join us for the upcoming What's New webcast on February 18, 2026 where we'll demo the highlights from the last few months. Sign up here: What’s New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2602.

Let's take a closer look at what’s new by business capability.

  • Asset Information and Data Management
  • Asset Risk Assessment
  • Asset Reliability Engineering
  • Asset Health Monitoring
  • Asset Performance Analysis

Asset Information and Data Management

Industrial Systems: From Manual Clicks to Scalable System Modeling

The challenge

System elements could be activated or deactivated, but only one at a time. There was no filtering or sorting to easily identify which elements were active or inactive. Excel uploads didn’t support activation or deactivation at all. For large system hierarchies, this made modeling slow, manual, and error-prone.

We wanted to provide reliability engineers with a faster, more scalable way to manage complex industrial systems — especially in environments with hundreds or thousands of system elements.

What’s new

Users can now:

  • Select multiple system elements and activate or deactivate them in one action
  • Filter and sort elements by activation status and other criteria
  • Use Excel uploads with a new Activate Element column to perform bulk updates

The outcome

  • Significantly reduced time and effort for system maintenance
  • Bulk operations that scale with system complexity
  • Improved usability for day-to-day modeling work

To read the full list of new features see here.
For more information,  see the SAP Help Portal:
  • Manage Industrial Systems
  • Activate or Deactivate a System Element
  • Export or Import a System Structure
  • Creating an Industrial System from Excel

Asset Risk Assessment

Risk & Criticality Assessments: From Forced Choices to Meaningful Classification

The challenge

Risk and Criticality Assessment Templates only supported a fixed set of resultant actions such as FMEA, RCM, OEM Guidelines, PM Review, and Run to Fail. In practice, not every assessment outcome fits neatly into one of these categories — leading to forced or misleading classifications.

Reliability engineers needed a way to document outcomes accurately, even when predefined actions didn’t apply.

What’s new

Templates now include two additional actions: “Others” and “Not Applicable.” This allows teams to capture generic or exceptional outcomes without misclassifying results.

New Actions ' Others' and 'Not Applicable' in app 'Manage Templates for Risk and Criticality Assessments''

The outcome:

  • More accurate and meaningful assessment documentation
  • Better decision-making by avoiding misclassification
  • Increased usability in varied operational contexts

Asset Reliability Engineering

Reliability Assessments: From Rigid Apps to an Extensible SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) Foundation

The challenge

Both the RCM Assessments and Strategy Assessments for Classes applications were built on a foundation that didn’t support field extensibility, timeline changes, or standard search and sort capabilities across list and object detail views. This made it difficult to tailor assessments to industry-specific needs, scale them across different asset classes, and deliver a consistent, modern user experience.

In practice, reliability teams were constrained by fixed data structures and limited UI flexibility — even though real-world reliability engineering demands customization, evolution, and long-term scalability.

Customers needed a future-ready foundation that supports customization, consistency, and continuous evolution — without forcing separate design patterns for different types of reliability assessments.

What’s new

SAP introduces new further CAP-based applications for both RCM Assessments and Strategy Assessments for Classes, built on the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) and OData APIs.

New entry points to new 'Manage RCM Assessments' and 'Manage Strategy Assessment for Classes' apps based on the SAP Cloud Application Programming framework

These new applications provide:

  • Out-of-the-box extensibility for adding custom fields. For more information, see the Extensibility Guide on the SAP Help Portal.
  • A more intuitive, consistent, and efficient user experience
  • A centralized foundation for industry-specific data capture and customizations

Both applications are replacing their current versions after a transition period.

The outcome:

  • Greater flexibility to tailor assessments to asset classes and industries
  • Improved usability and consistency across reliability workflows
  • A scalable, future-proof architecture for ongoing innovation

For more information on release timelines, implementation, support, and known limitations, refer to SAP Note 3603407.

Read also this blog by Archi Garg on extensibility Extending SAP Asset Performance Management: Three Approaches to Tailoring Your Asset Operations

 

RCM Consequence Evaluation: New Custom Questionnaires

The challenge

Until now, the Manage RCM Assessments application supported consequence evaluation using a default questionnaire. While this provided a standardized starting point, it didn’t reflect the reality that organizations operate in different industries, regulatory environments, and risk contexts. A generic questionnaire often limited the relevance and effectiveness of RCM assessments.

Customers needed a way to align consequence evaluation with their own operational and risk management strategies — without being constrained by a fixed structure.

What’s new

Users can now configure the RCM consequence evaluation questionnaire to match organizational needs. Administrators and reliability engineers can define their own evaluation framework for technical objects, making RCM assessments more context-aware and meaningful.

How to configure an RCM consequence evaluation in the Configuration Manager and the custom questionnaire in the 'Manage RCM Assessments' app

The outcome

  • Greater process flexibility
  • Industry- and context-specific consequence evaluations
  • More effective and relevant RCM results

 To learn how to create a custom questionnaire read the SAP Help Portal Custom Questionnaires.

Assessment Validity Periods: From Static Analyses to Living Assessments

The challenge

There was no way to define a validity period for RCM assessments and Strategy Assessments for Classes, therefore there was no built-in mechanism to track when they should be revisited or to align recommendations with assessment relevance.

Customers needed a way to ensure assessments stay current and to systematically trigger reviews at the right time.

What’s new

Users can now assign start and end dates to assessments. Validity periods can be propagated to linked recommendations, and automatic tracking and notifications alert teams when an assessment is due for review.

New validity period fields in the 'Manage Strategy Assessments for Classes' app

The outcome

  • Clear definition and tracking of assessment relevance
  • Improved visibility through date-based filtering
  • Consistent recommendations aligned with assessment validity

Asset Health Monitoring

Rule Processing: From Static Aggregates to Time-Aware Decisions

The challenge

Condition-based rules supported aggregates like last, first, and previous, but without any time-window context. This meant rules couldn’t evaluate data within a bounded time range, limiting both rule definition and the accuracy of decisions based on historical values.

Customers needed finer control over how rules interpret data — especially in scenarios where recent trends matters more than long-term values.

What’s new

Aggregate functions now support optional time windows. Rules can evaluate last, first, and previous values within a defined period, such as the last hour or day. This ensures rule evaluations are based on the most relevant data and enables more accurate, context-aware decisions.

An example rule showing how temperature conditions can be evaluated using window-based aggregate values to trigger a high-temperature alert

The outcome:

  • More accurate and context-aware decisions
  • Improved operational efficiency
  • Greater flexibility in rule design

For more information, see the SAP Help Portal Working with Aggregates.

 

Asset Performance Analysis

Embedded Analytics in APM: Solving Reliability Problems Faster

Embedded analytics dashboards—including the option to link external SAP Analytics Cloud dashboards—are now available for embedded IoT tenants.
For reliability engineers, this means answers faster, in context, and without jumping between tools.

As a reliability engineer, I need to:

  • Understand which assets are driving risk
  • Identify patterns behind failures and performance degradation
  • Validate whether corrective actions are actually working

The challenge is rarely a lack of data—it’s connecting the right data to the right asset at the right moment.

See asset data together

Embedded analytics dashboards provide interactive visualizations across technical objects in SAP Asset Performance Management.
They combine multiple assessment types into meaningful dashboards, exposing key indicators alongside risks, liabilities, and alerts—all within the asset management context, not in a separate reporting tool.

Working with the data

Configurable charts and tables based on analytical data objects support analysis at both summary and detail level.
This allows comparison of similar assets, identifying recurring failure patterns, and prioritizing issues based on impact, not volume.

Creating dashboards

Dashboards can be created and adapted directly in APM using embedded SAP Analytics Cloud.
As investigations evolve, I can adjust KPIs, filters, and visualizations without leaving APM or waiting for reports, supporting guided exploration of data from overview to detail.

Connecting to external SAC dashboards

When broader or cross-domain analysis is required, external SAP Analytics Cloud dashboards can be linked to embedded analytics dashboards in APM. 
For this, a separate SAP Analytics Cloud license is required.

For more information, see the SAP Help Portal Manage Analytics Dashboards.

 

Stay Informed and Get Involved

  • Don't forget to sign up for the upcoming webcast on February 18, 2026  What’s New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2602
  • You can also explore our latest blog posts here, including topics such as Embedded IoT by @florian_weigold, Industrial Systems by @SonamKinger, and the extensibility blog by @archigarg mentioned earlier.
  • Find out what's up and coming in the SAP Road Map Explorer.
  • For an exhaustive list of all new features, visit the SAP Help Portal - What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management.. 
  • Send us your improvement requests. Submit or vote for the improvement you would like to see in the SAP Continuous Influence session.
  • Check the January update of the APM link collection.

entry.title: Benefits of Maintenance Planning Overview Fiori Application
entry.content:
In the fast-paced world of industrial asset management, the difference between a high-performing plant and a costly bottleneck often comes down to how quickly a maintenance planner can identify and react to issues.

In the legacy SAP environment, this meant digging through multiple transaction codes (T-codes) to find overdue orders or missing parts. With SAP S/4HANA, the Maintenance Planning Overview (App ID: F2828) Fiori application changes the game by consolidating critical data into a single, proactive dashboard.

Here is an overview of why this app is becoming the "command center" for modern maintenance planners.

What is the Maintenance Planning Overview App?

The Maintenance Planning Overview is an analytical Fiori application designed as a "one-stop shop" for Maintenance Planners. Built on the SAP Fiori Overview Page (OVP) floorplan, it uses interactive "cards" to display high-level KPIs and real-time data visualizations.

Instead of searching for problems, the problems are pushed to you. The app analyzes factors like open notifications, missing spare parts, and overdue orders based on a reference period (e.g., "This Week" or "Next Week") that you define.

Key Features: Your Planning Toolkit

The app is organized into task-oriented cards. Each card serves a specific purpose in the maintenance lifecycle:

1. Planning & Backlog Management

  • Orders for Planning: Identifies maintenance orders that have been created but not yet released. It visualizes these by priority or order type using pie charts.

  • Outstanding Notifications: Shows notifications that haven't been assigned to an order yet, allowing you to catch work requests before they fall through the cracks.

2. Material & Procurement Visibility

  • Purchase Requisitions/Orders Not Approved: Tracks non-stock materials required for maintenance. You can see which parts might cause delays because the requisition hasn't been approved or the purchase order hasn't been sent.

  • Non-Stock Materials Not Available: A critical card that flags ordered parts that will not arrive by the required start date of the maintenance order.

3. Execution Tracking

  • Overdue Orders: Highlights released orders where the end date has passed but the operations haven't been finally confirmed.

  • Orders Not Technically Completed: Provides a list of orders that are physically finished but haven't been closed out in the system, which is vital for accurate financial reporting.

 

 

The app typically includes the following standard Fiori cards:

1. Maintenance Order Cards

  • Orders for Planning: Visualizes maintenance orders that have been created but not yet released. It usually breaks them down by Priority or Order Type.

  • Overdue Orders: Analyzes released maintenance orders where the basic end date has already passed, but the operations have not been finally confirmed.

  • Orders for Completion: Specifically highlights orders where all operations are confirmed (CNF) but the order header has not yet been set to Technically Completed (TECO).

  • Jobs (Order Operations): Displays a list of upcoming or current maintenance "jobs" (operations) that require attention or execution within the defined reference period.

2. Maintenance Notification Cards

  • Outstanding Notifications: Displays notifications that have been created but are not yet assigned to a maintenance order. This helps prevent a backlog of unaddressed work requests.

  • Recently Created Notifications: Gives a quick look at the newest requests entering the system, categorized by their processing status.

3. Procurement & Material Cards

  • Non-Stock Materials Not Available: This is a high-value card that flags non-stock components (parts ordered specifically for an order) that are not expected to arrive by the order's start date.

  • Purchase Requisitions Not Approved: Identifies PRs linked to maintenance orders that are stalled in the approval workflow, which could delay planned repairs.

  • Purchase Requisitions Not Converted: Shows approved requisitions that have not yet been turned into formal Purchase Orders (POs).

4. Technical Object Cards

  • Technical Object Damages: Often used to visualize which assets are experiencing the most frequent issues, categorized by damage codes or object parts.

     

Key Features of These Cards

  • Filter Sensitivity: All cards react to the global filters at the top of the app (e.g., Planning Plant, Planner Group, or Time Period).

  • Drill-Down Capability: Clicking the header of a card takes you to a detailed list view (like the "Manage Maintenance Orders" app), while clicking a specific data point (like a slice of a pie chart) filters that list further.

  • Personalization: Users can hide cards they don't need or rearrange them on the dashboard to put the most critical KPIs at the top

entry.title: A Practical Solution for Multiple Counter Maintenance Plans During SAP PM Data Migration
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

This blog explains how Multiple Counter Maintenance Plans can be uploaded in mass during data migration. We all know that standard tools like LTMC and LSMW is not suitable for Multiple Counter Plans. The upload program is created using a Function Module MPLAN_CREATE. The FM internally creates data in tables such as MPLA, MPOS, and MMPT. The main focus is that we developed in a way that it derives cycle values directly from the cycle set (strategy) and dynamically populating the cycle structure in this FM. This approach made the creation automatic of this complex maintenance plan upload and an alignment with configuration.

BUSINESS REQUIREMENT AND CHALLENGE

We had a business requirement which was to create multiple counter maintenance plans based on cycle sets. During the migration phase, we faced the issue that this requirement proved to be more complex than expected because there is no standard LTMC template available for uploading multiple counter maintenance plans, and LSMW was also not feasible, because it cannot be recorded if there is a scroll as the cycle data becomes difficult to manage when a strategy contains more than four cycle lines. Manual creation was not an option due to the volume of data and the complexity of the cycle definitions.

To address this limitation, we developed a Custom Upload Program using a standard Function Module MPLAN_CREATE. This blog describes how this FM was structured and, how cycle values were automatically derived from the cycle set and populated into the cycle structure of the FM.

MAIN STRUCTURES USED

This Function Module contains the following structures, and we used those structures to develop the upload program. 

  • HEADER – Maintenance plan header
  • ITEM – Maintenance plan items
  • CYCLE – Cycle definition
  • RETURN – Messages

WHY MPLAN_CREATE?

We opted for MPLAN_CREATE because this FM provides full control over Maintenance plan header, Maintenance plan items and Cycle definitions. Instead of hardcoding the cycles, we wanted the system to always read cycle values directly from IP11Z. That way, the upload program would remain stable even if cycle sets change in the future.

OVERALL DESIGN IDEA

The program follows a simple logical flow. That is 

  • First, the maintenance plan header is prepared.
  • Next, the maintenance plan items are prepared.
  • Finally, the cycle data is derived from the cycle set and filled into the Function module cycle table.

INPUT PARAMETERS FOR HEADER

The header structure defines how the plan behaves. We defined only the required scheduling-related fields.

Key fields used in our program:

  • WPTXT – Maintenance plan text
  • ABRHO – Scheduling period
  • MPTYP – Maintenance plan category
  • HUNIT – Unit for Scheduling Period
  • PLAN_SORT – Sort field for maintenance plans

INPUT PARAMETERS FOR MAINTENANCE PLAN ITEMS 

For every maintenance plan there are multiple items linked to the same equipment, but with different task list counters. For every item, we filled the item number, equipment, task list type, task list group, and group counter. Order type, maintenance activity type, and priority were also passed.

One fascinating part here was the work center. The FM does not accept the work center name directly. It accepts the Object ID (OBJID). So, we derived it dynamically by passing the work center and plant into table CRHD and fetching the corresponding OBJID. This value was then assigned to the item structure. The below fields are defined in the item

  • WPPOS – Item number
  • PSTXT – Item text
  • EQUNR – Equipment
  • PLNTY, PLNNR, PLNAL – Task list assignment
  • IWERK – Plant
  • AUART – Order type
  • ILART – Maintenance activity type
  • PRIOK – Priority

WORK CENTER DERIVATION

The work center (GEWRK) cannot be passed directly by its name. So, the work center required by the FM was derived dynamically by using the below logic,

  • Input: Pass Work center (CRHD-ARBL) and Plant (CRHD-WERKS)
  • Output: Fetch the Object ID (CRHD-OBJID)

This OBJID was passed to the MPLAN_CREATE item structure.

READ AND INPUT THE CYCLE SET (STRATEGY)

Our upload program was developed in a way that cycle set is automatically fetched instead of entering cycle values manually. We passed the Cycle set name to table T351P. This table stores the parameters of each strategy, including the cycle values and units.

Input: Cycle Set Name which was created using IP11Z

Table used: T351P (Maintenance Packages)

Data Fetched from T351P

  • ZAEHL – Cycle number
  • ZYKZT – Cycle value
  • ZEIEH – Unit of measure
  • KTEX1 – Short text

By doing this, we ensured that the program always uses the same cycle definition as maintained in IP11Z. For every line found in T351P, one line was created in the MPLAN_CREATE cycle table CYCLE.

The fields were filled as follows:

  • The cycle number was taken from the cycle set sequence.
  • The cycle value was taken from the cycle definition in the cycle set.
  • The unit of measure was taken from the cycle set unit.
  • The cycle text was taken from the cycle set short text.

This mapping paved us to create multiple cycles for the same maintenance plan without manual entry.

Fields populated:

  • NUMBER → From T351P-ZAEHL
  • ZYKL1→ From T351P-KTEX1(fetch the first word, as the value is entered in the first word of the text in cycle set)
  • ZEIEH → From T351P-ZEIEH
  • PAK_TEXT → From T351P-KTEX1

COUNTER POINT DETERMINATION

As this was a counter-based maintenance plan, we had to determine the counter point dynamically. The equipment number was first passed into EQUI table to fetch the object number. That object number was then passed into IMPTT table to fetch the counter point. We selected the counter by filtering the characteristics name which represented the operating hours. This counter point was then assigned to the relevant cycle entries in CYCLE. This step ensured that the cycle was linked to the correct measurement counter of the equipment.

  1. Pass EQUNR → table EQUI → Fetch OBJNR
  2. Pass OBJNR → table IMPTT → Fetch POINT → Filter where ATINN = 'HOURS'

This POINT is passed into CYCLE-POINT.

SEQUENCE AND REPETITION LOGIC

To support multiple counter groups, we used:

  • CYCLESEQIND to define the cycle group
  • SETREPEATIND to indicate repetition

OUTCOME

The outcome of this approach is, 

  • Multiple counter maintenance plans were created automatically
  • Cycle data was always aligned with strategy configuration
  • No dependency on LTMC or LSMW was required
  • Manual creation effort was eliminated

The solution proved to be stable, reusable, and fully controlled.

CONCLUSION

MPLAN_CREATE becomes a practical and powerful alternative when standard migration tools do not support complex PM scenarios such as Multiple Counter Maintenance Plans with cycle sets. By deriving cycle values from the table T351P and counter points from the table IMPTT, we were able to automate the creation of upload program for Multiple Counter Maintenance Plan, while keeping the logic aligned with SAP configuration. This approach helped us to bridge the gap between functional configuration and technical automation in a clean and maintainable way.

entry.title: Replicate SAP S/4HANA Measuring Points and Measurement Documents to SAP Field Service Management
entry.content:
In asset-intensive industries, equipment health isn’t just a metric—it’s the backbone of operational efficiency. Organizations running SAP S/4HANA often use Measuring Points and Measurement Documents to monitor equipment performance and condition. These data points help ensure timely maintenance and prevent costly downtime.

But here’s the challenge:
When field technicians work in SAP Field Service Management (FSM), they often lack access to these critical insights. Without real-time data in the field, service teams face delays, reactive maintenance, and frustrated customers.

So, what’s the solution?
Seamless integration between SAP S/4HANA and SAP FSM. By replicating Measuring Points and Measurement Documents from S/4HANA to FSM, technicians gain real-time visibility into asset health—right where the work happens.

Why Does This Integration Matter?

Imagine this scenario:

  • A technician is dispatched to repair a pump.
  • In FSM, they see the service order but have no clue about the pump’s recent performance trends.
  • They spend extra time diagnosing issues that could have been avoided if they had access to measurement readings.

With integration:

  • The technician sees the latest pressure readings, temperature and vibration trends in FSM.
  • They arrive prepared, diagnose faster, and fix proactively.
  • Result? Less downtime, happier customers, and lower costs.

Key Benefits of Integration

  • Real-time synchronization of equipment performance data between S/4HANA and FSM.
  • Improved visibility for technicians in the field.
  • Proactive service delivery, leading to higher customer satisfaction.

Scope of the Integration

Here’s what’s included in the S/4HANA On-Premise October 2025 release.

  • Measuring Point Replication
    • Create or update measuring points in S/4HANA.
    • Changes automatically replicate to FSM for the corresponding equipment.
  • Measurement Document Replication
    • Measurement readings or counters created in S/4HANA replicate to FSM.
    • Readings can also be captured during maintenance order confirmation.
    • Measurement readings captured in FSM replicate back to S/4HANA is available via SAP Note: 3684637 - Implementation of Replication Logic for Measurement Document from SAP Field Service Management to SAP S/4HANA
  • Initial and Delta Loads
    • Supports both bulk initial load and incremental updates.
  • Multi-company Support
    • Data replication can be configured for multiple FSM companies.

Prerequisites

  • For Measuring Points in FSM:
    • Measurement Point Category (handled by integration).
    • Equipment and Characteristics (already managed via MAIF framework).
  • For Measurement Documents in FSM:
    • The corresponding Measuring Point must exist in FSM.

Configuration Steps

Delta Replication

  • For single FSM company:
    Navigate to SPRO → Service → Integration with Other SAP Solutions → SAP Field Service Management → Configure Integration

Maintain following settings (Use the same RFC destination as Equipment replication.):

 

  • For multiple FSM companies:
    SPRO → Service → Integration with Other SAP Solutions → SAP Field Service Management → Configure Multiple Company Replication

Define S/4HANA FSM Company:

 

Assig FSM Company Details:

 

 

Assign Replication Object Details:

 

  • Create BGRFC inbound destination:
    Transaction: SBGRFCCONF

 

 

  • Extract AIF Contents:
    Transaction: /AIF/CONTENT_EXTRACT

 

 

Initial Load

  • Go to DRFIMG → Define Custom Settings for Data Replication → Define Technical Settings for Business Systems.

 

Maintain Business System and Logical System (Ignore if already created):

 

Define Business Systems, BOs:

 

Define Business Systems, Bos, Communication Channel:

 

Maintain the above settings for BO Type 5236 as well.

 

  • Define Replication Models:

Go to DRFIMG -> Define Custom Settings for Data Replication -> Define Replication Models

 

Create following replication models:

 

             

Maintain the following settings for Replication Model MDOC_REPL:

 

 

 

 

 

 

Maintain the abovementioned settings for Replication Model MP_REPL as well.

 

Delta replication Demo

Scenario: Wind energy operator: Each turbine has measuring point for running hours. This points (created on the equipment/functional location in S/4HANA) is replicate to FSM. When maintenance technician performs the service then maintenance measurement readings are captured during maintenance order confirmation. These readings are also replicated to FSM.

Create Measuring Point: tcode: IK01

 

 

Check the measuring point replication log in S/4HANA

Tcode /AIF/ERR

 

 

 

Check the replication in FSM:

Note: Measuring Points and Measurement documents can be viewed with in the equipment using Mobile App. At present it cannot be viewed via FSM website.

 

 

Create a maintenance order for the maintenance of the Wind Turbine and assign the Measuring point as PRT

 

 

Create maintenance order confirmation using tcode: IW41 and create measurement document

 

 

Measurement document can also be viewed in tcode IK13

 

 

Check the measurement document replication log in S/4HANA

Tcode /AIF/ERR

 

 

 

For more details, visit https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/738a456365c0414faba6426d05fd8674/0ccd50bd60554bfe90af11864497f5de.html?locale=en-US.

Final Thoughts

Integrating Measuring Points and Measurement Documents between SAP S/4HANA and FSM isn’t just a technical upgrade—it’s a strategic move toward data-driven field service. With real-time insights, technicians can work smarter, customers stay happier, and businesses save money.

entry.title: SAP Build Process Automation(SBPA):S4HANA Asset Management-Automating Major Maintenance Work Request
entry.content:
This blog provides a broad outline of how SAP Business Process Automation can help maintenance teams streamline the "Major Maintenance Work" Request Form, including the Sanction Committee Governance approval process.

What is SAP Build Process Automation?

SAP Build Process Automation (SBPA) is a low-code, no-code service on the SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform) that enables users to automate, manage, and optimize business processes and workflows without writing code.

We can start the business process in four ways: using a request form or an API or an Event or a Scheduled Trigger.

 

My Business Use Case:

My maintenance team is preparing for a major equipment overhaul. They must present the business case with the supporting evidence to the Sanction Committee Governance for FY26 budget approval. Once the sanction committee approves the budget, the maintenance team initiates the maintenance order process.

In this blog post, I will outline the process of creating the major maintenance work request form and explain how the approval/rejection workflow operates. (Note: The automatic creation of maintenance order at the end of the approval process will be outlined in my next blog post).

For my business use case in this blog post, the process is triggered when a form is submitted. The process then continues with a combination of steps, such as Approval Forms, Email notifications until it reaches its end.

 

If you want to gain insights into how SAP JOULE skill works, please check out my latest blog posts below.

  • SAP JOULE & ME : My First JOULE Skill in SAP EHSM-Incident Management Without Coding 
  • SAP Joule Skill for Technical Completion of Maintenance Orders in S/4HANA Asset Management 

 

We will first create a business process project using SAP Build Lobby. Then, we will design the business process. Once the business process is completed, we will create and configure forms. After that, we will create and configure approval form, and finally, we will configure the email notification.

Once we complete the setup, the final result looks like this.

Let us first create a project using SAP Build Process Automation.

Business process projects are a collection of skills in SAP Build Process Automation. Projects are part of the internal business processes of a company and are defined based on business scenarios. A project can contain a set of processes, forms, automations and decisions, which are reusable artifacts.

 

Inside a project, we can create a process. We can create a business process version using a combination of artifacts (such as forms and decisions) and process controls (such as branches, conditions, and Mail Notifications).

Let us now add forms to the business process. For Instance let us use "Form" to trigger the business process.

Let us design the form using "Open Editor".

for instance, let us add Input fields with labels.

  • General Information: we can add the dropdown values wherever required, so that the maintenance team can select the desired value while submitting the form.

  • Scope Of Work

  • Cost Estimate : Projected Cost for Materials and Services

Now the trigger form is ready. Let us design the approval process form.

The approval form is used to get faster and easier approvals from the business users. These forms are converted into tasks in an automated workflow which will appear in the My Inbox of the user. We can design and modify without coding.

Let us design the Approval Form in the form builder by dragging and dropping fields into the form editor and configure the field settings. As the L1 Approver does not have to modify the data, let us make the fields "Read Only" for the L1 approver and add a new field to input L1 Approver Comments.

 

Now, let us configure the L1 Approval form and map the fields. We can use bind list wherever required. The subject that we mention in general section will be displayed when this task appears in "My Inbox". And this task will be appeared in the "My Inbox" of the provided "Recipient".

Similarly, let us create an L2 Approver Form. Let us use the option "Duplicate".

Duplicate feature is used to copy artifacts within the business process. The copy feature is used to copy artifacts across the business process. 

Similarly, let us configure the general information and map the input fields.

We have just completed the approval form for the major maintenance work request process.

My organization demands an email notification to be sent to the requestor whenever there is an action, i.e., Approve or Rejection happens on the Major Maintenance Work Request Form.

Let us configure the Header and Subject of the E-Mail for the "Rejection" process.

Now, let us configure the E-Mail Body.

Similarly, let us configure the Header, Subject and Body of the E-Mail Notification for the "Approval" process.

Once the Major Maintenance Work Request Form with two levels of Approvals  is completed, we can release and deploy it in the respective environment.

Now, Let us run the business process...

Business Case Details:

Scope Of Work Details:

Cost Estimate Details:

Now let us submit the form. 

The L1 Approver can now check "My Inbox"  to review the Major Maintenance Work Request Form.

Since the L1 Approver Comments are mandatory for this form, the option "Approve" or "Rejection" will be highlighted once L1 Approver provides the comments.

Once the Major Maintenance Request Form is approved by the L1 Approver, It will be displayed in L2 Approver`s Inbox. Let us approve it and send an email notification to the requestor upon the approval. L1 Approver comments are displayed while L2 Approver approves or rejects the form.

Once the workflow is ended, we can monitor the processes.

The monitoring page collects the events coming from the deployed workflows and automation jobs and present them to the user. These events represents the status of the jobs that have been run, and the business activity of the monitoring events.

For my business use case, we can view the process, workflow status using "Process and Workflow Instances" application.

😊This brings me to the end of process walkthrough on SAP Build Process Automation using forms as a trigger for one of the use cases, i.e., Major Maintenance Work Request.

In Summary,  we have designed and configured a digital form to capture structured data for a "Major Maintenance Work". Implemented multi level approvals (L1- L2). And also triggered automated email notifications to the requestor, improving transparency and reducing manual efforts. This entire approach established a foundation that can be scaled up and showed how automation can reduce repetitive tasks and speeds up the approval cycle with more transparency.

Stay tuned for more updates on SAP Build Process Automation and SAP Joule Skills/Agents in S/4HANA Asset Management Space...

-- 

Yours Sincerely,

Pardhasaradhi Reddy. C

entry.title: Automating Price Updates from MM Contracts into Task Lists (SAP PM): A Pragmatic Deep Dive
entry.content:
Business Problem

Maintenance organizations often run recurring plan/task list operations with service lines (ESLL). Prices are maintained centrally in MM service contracts (EKPO/ESLH/ESLL + PRS condition). Without automation, updating those prices across all task lists is a manual grind — and highly error‑prone.

Requirement: Get current contract prices into task list service lines without manually touching every ESLL row.

Solution: The report reads current PRS conditions (A081 → KONP) per LV leaf from the contract structure and updates the corresponding ESLL price rows in the maintenance task list — deterministically mapped via EXTROW.




What the Report Does (Functional Flow)

1) Discover the Contract’s LV Leaves

  • Resolve the root PACKNO from EKPO (PSTYP = 9).
  • Traverse the ESLL hierarchy using SUB_PACKNO and collect only leaf rows (non‑packages).
  • Extract for each leaf: PACKNO, INTROW, EXTROW, SRVPOS, KTEXT1.

Why leaves?
They’re the price‑bearing service lines that map 1:1 into the task list.

2) Read Current Conditions from A081/KONP

  • For each leaf, pull A081 using (KAPPL, KSCHL, KONT_PACK, KONT_ZEILE, WERKS, DATBI).
  • From KONP, read:
    • KBETR (unit price)
    • KONWA (currency)
    • KPEIN (price unit)
    • KMEIN (price unit UoM)

Build an internal price table keyed by EXTROW for fast lookup via binary search.

3) Load the Task List ESLL Rows

  • Use PLAS/PLPO → ESLH/ESLL (or MPOS as fallback) to fetch all relevant ESLL rows.
  • Bring currency context from ESLH-WAERS.
  • Optionally restrict by operation number VORNR.

4) Update Prices Consistently

Join logic

Match contract prices to task list lines via EXTROW.

Guardrails

  • Currency must match (KONWA == ESLH-WAERS).
  • PEINH and MENGE must be present.
  • If required data is missing → skip safely.

  • Pricing math

    NETWR/BRTWR = KBETR * (MENGE / PEINH)

    • Optional single price (TBTWR)

      Set only if units match (KMEIN == MEINS

      TBTWR = KBETR * (ESLL-PEINH / KONP-KPEIN)

      Optional MPOS header price

      MPOS-NETPR = KBETR

  • Modes

    • Simulation (default): no DB writes, full trace.
    • Real run: commits changes and summarizes results.

 

 

Why EXTROW Is the Right Mapping Key

  • Stability: More robust than internal row numbers when LV structures change.
  • Clarity: Business and IT can rely on one rule:
    “If EXTROW is copied from contract to task list, prices map 1:1.”
  • Performance: Enables sorting + binary search instead of fuzzy matching.



Key Technical Elements:

PARAMETERS: p_ebeln TYPE ekko-ebeln OBLIGATORY,   " Contract number
            p_ebelp TYPE ekpo-ebelp,              " Optional item
            p_werks TYPE werks_d    OBLIGATORY,   " Plant for A081
            p_kschl TYPE konp-kschl DEFAULT 'PRS',
            p_kappl TYPE konh-kappl DEFAULT 'MS'.

PARAMETERS: p_plnty TYPE plko-plnty DEFAULT 'A' OBLIGATORY, " Task list type
            p_plnnr TYPE plko-plnnr OBLIGATORY,             " Group
            p_plnal TYPE plko-plnal OBLIGATORY,             " Group counter
            p_vornr TYPE plpo-vornr.                        " Optional op

PARAMETERS: p_test   AS CHECKBOX DEFAULT 'X', " Simulation
            p_dbg    AS CHECKBOX DEFAULT 'X', " Debug/trace
            p_setmp  AS CHECKBOX,             " Set MPOS-NETPR
            p_settbw AS CHECKBOX DEFAULT 'X'. " Set ESLL-TBTWR​

 

Core Calculations

lv_scale     = gs_out_tl-menge / gs_out_tl-peinh.
lv_new_netwr = ls_price-kbetr * lv_scale.     " ESLL-NETWR/BRTWR

" Optional TBTWR (single price), only if units align:
IF ls_price-kmein = gs_out_tl-meins.
  lv_factor     = (gs_out_tl-peinh / ls_price-kpein) OR 1 if KPEIN = 0
  lv_tbtwr_new  = ls_price-kbetr * lv_factor.
ENDIF.

 

  • Built‑In Safeguards

    • Currency check (avoids accidental conversions).
    • Quantity/unit checks (avoid division by zero).
    • Unit congruence enforced for TBTWR.
    • Simulation mode gives trace + predictable dry-runs.
    • Counters summarize:
      • found leaves
      • A081 hits
      • KONP reads
      • task list lines
      • updates vs. skips
      • MPOS/TBTWR writes



Conclusion

The report solves a high‑friction maintenance problem with a precise, auditable mechanism:
it maps current contract prices into task list service lines using a stable external key (EXTROW), respects unit/currency safeguards, and allows safe simulation-first execution.

The full ABAP code can be found on my github: https://github.com/ProjectNautilus001/ABAP-Solutions-for-SAP-PM/blob/main/Y_PM_WPLAN_PREISUPDATE

entry.title: New in SAP EAM with S/4HANA 2025 FPS01: Task Planning Management API
entry.content:
Task Planning Management in S/4HANA Asset Management - SAP Media Share

 

Hello EAM community!

I’m Michael Spiess, team architect and scrum master of the Wall/E team. I’m excited to share our team’s first S/4HANA delivery — and it’s a big one!

What’s new:

  • Task Planning Management API: Programmatic access to roughly 90% of Compatible Unit Management capabilities. 
  • New feature for the Process Maintenance Order app (W0017). You can now assign a compatible unit design to a maintenance order. 

Enjoy exploring the new API, and trying out the app enhancement, and stay tuned — more updates from the Wall/E team are on the way!

 

What is Task Planning Management?

Task Planning Management (TPM) is the successor of Compatible Unit Management.

The primary reason for this rebranding is to emphasize that the compatible unit functionality will be enhanced to serve not only the utility industry but also other sectors.

These enhancements are planned for future releases; in the current release, the new term is only applicable to the APIs. 

 

What is Compatible Unit Management?

Compatible Unit Management is an enterprise extension in SAP EAM for organizations that construct and maintain physical assets using standard, repeatable units of work. With compatible units (CUs), you define a reusable work package for an asset or asset component and enrich it with job-specific details, such as:

  • Job steps and planned duration
  • Crew and skill requirements
  • Special tools and materials
  • Reference documents
  • Accounting and cost assignments

CUs are modular by design and typically mirror the engineering structure (EBOM) of an asset. They’re often authored in CAD systems and then transferred to SAP EAM to bring the CU design to life — from resource allocation for execution through to financial settlement.

For example, one CU might define a utility pole, and another might define the installation of crossarms on that pole. Multiple CUs can be bundled into a CU design document to represent the entire project, for example to build a utility connection for a new construction site.  In a planning phase you may be interested to discuss design variants before you decide together with the customer for the best option. This is why you can assign multiple design documents to a construction measure document and release the selected design document from there. The construction measure can be cross-linked with a WBS element, a notification, or a master maintenance order.

 

How do Task Planning Management and Compatible Unit Management relate to each other?

The new Task Planning Management API creates and updates compatible unit master data and has full access to the two documents in Compatible Unit Management:

  • Compatible Unit Design
  • Construction Measure

This diagram shows how the new business objects of Task Planning Management - built with latest RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) technology -  get full access to master data and documents from databases of the Compatible Unit domain.

Figure 1: Relationship between Task Planning Management and Compatible Unit Management

 

 

End users can still use Compatible Unit Management in SAP GUI while developers can already use the new business objects to access Compatible Unit Management functionality from API version 1.0.0 onwards.

 

What are the benefits of Task Planning Management?

 

Shorter design cycles

The Task Planning Management API streamlines the conversion of an engineering bill of material (EBOM) into a task planning design document which represents the  service bill of material (SBOM). CAD developers can easily consume the TPM OData V4 API as a web-extension. This to offers engineers  a seamless app experience, enabling them to create a task planning design document on the fly while working on the engineering bill of material.  Once the engineering design document is released, the task planning design document is simultaneously released. This becomes a single, reusable source of truth that maintenance planners can embed directly in maintenance orders for resource planning, execution, maintenance and financial settlement.

The following diagram shows  in three detail levels how CAD objects are mapped to the corresponding business objects of Task Planning Management:

Figure 2: EBOM to SBOM transformation example with Task Planning Management API

Enhanced work order efficiency

Whether you assign a task planning element to a reusable design document or ad-hoc to a maintenance order, selecting the right operations for each part of the build becomes straightforward. Thanks to a modular structure and inheritance from the parent planning element, data entry is minimal. In the following example the user inputs two values ("install", "classic") to estimate time and costs for a classic pole installation:

Figure 3: classification system and multi-level value inheritance boosts work efficiency

More accurate job estimates

When Operation Account Assignment (OAA) is enabled, you can track actuals down to each task planning element. This granular visibility allows teams to fine-tune planned costs for specific planning elements, resulting in more accurate quotes and better cost predictability across projects.

 

How will Task Planning Management evolve in the future?

Let’s take a look ahead! Please keep in mind that these are my personal views. For the most reliable view of what’s coming, explore SAP Road Maps at https://roadmaps.sap.com/ and search for “EAM.”

One of the hottest topics I see is the integration of Task Planning Management with SAP S/4HANA Service Management. Having a task planning design for execution and maintenance is fine, but using this design document easily in a quotation process doubles the value of Task Planning Management!

Integration with SAP Project System (PS) is another win. With precise durations and work centers derived from the design, project managers can confidently schedule construction phases and identify the optimal slots in the overall project plan. Bringing in SAP Geographical Enablement Framework (GEF) would be equally powerful. It would allow maintenance planners to search for installed planning elements and designs on a map, accelerating decisions and improving asset insights.

To tie these capabilities together, SAP’s AI assistant, Joule, is the natural companion! With Task Planning Management skills, Joule lets users adjust designs on the fly and guide them straight to the screens where those changes impact quote values and project timelines.

Figure 4: Task Planning Management agent communication

 

How can I try out the API?

 

Prerequisites

  • Business function for Compatible Unit Management switched on
  • Initial configuration for Compatible Unit Management completed
  • Permission to new authorization objects for Task Planning Management (I_TPMTPE, I_TPMDSGN, I_TPMCM) granted
  • Access to transaction /IWFND/GW_CLIENT 

 

Overview about the process - from task planning element via design to the new app feature

Start exploring the Task Planning Management API by creating your first task planning element in a few steps! Once the first task planning element is created, valuated, and released, you can further enhance it to a multi-level task planning element to represent a classic utility pole, like the pole from figure 3. You can use the pole in a new design document to test your first task planning element. To test out all three business objects you can create a construction measure and release the design from there. Use the new feature "Assign CU-Design" in app W0017 "Process Maintenance Order" to verify the successful execution of the request.

Here are the required requests in an overview:

Step

API request

How many times

1.0

Create task planning element 

3x (pole, cross arm, insulator)

1.1

Create value assignment

3x (pole, cross arm, insulator)

1.2

Release task planning element

3x (pole, cross arm, insulator)

1.3

Create task planning element BOM item

2x (pole, cross arm)

2.0

Create task planning design document

1x

2.1

Create the value assignment for the utility pole

1x

3.0

Create construction measure and release design 

1x

 

Step 1.0: Create a task planning element in 7 steps 

Let us create the first task planning element for an 40 ft wooden utility pole:

1.  SAP Logon to the S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 system

2. Call transaction /IWFND/GW_CLIENT

3. Change the radio-button from GET to POST

4. Copy this line into the Request URI text box:

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_taskplanningelement/srvd_a2x/sap/taskplanningelement/0001/TaskPlanningElement

5. Copy these lines into the HTTP-Request body box and replace the values with your compatible unit configuration:

{
